meta.description: Wood Elf origin story — the last breath of the Verdant Reaches

welf_001.title: The Last Green Memory
text: You close your eyes and the forest comes back. It always comes back.

The Verdant Reaches. Three hundred thousand acres of old-growth timber that predated human civilization by millennia. Canopy so thick the rain never touched the ground directly — it filtered down through a billion leaves, arriving as mist that tasted of chlorophyll and centuries. Your people called it the Breathing World, because the forest had its own rhythms, its own slow pulse that matched the heartbeat of any elf who stood still long enough to listen.

You can still feel it. Eighty-seven years since the burning, and the phantom sensation of roots beneath your feet wakes you most mornings before the alarm. The therapist at the refugee processing center called it ecological grief disorder. Your grandmother had a better name for it: the Hollowing.

The concrete walls of your room in Sector 7 press close. The recycled air tastes of engine grease and human sweat. Somewhere above you, through fourteen stories of stacked housing and corroded ductwork, the acid rain hammers the surface of a city that was built on the ashes of everything you loved.

choices.text: Let the memory take you back to the forest
Force your eyes open — the past is a poison
Reach for the carved wooden token under your pillow

welf_002.title: The Breathing World
text: The memory unfolds like a flower opening in time-lapse. You were young — barely forty, still a sapling by elven reckoning. The forest canopy above you was a cathedral of living green, every branch and leaf connected to every other through a root network the elders called the Whisper Web. When a tree fell in the eastern reaches, elves on the western border felt it in their bones.

You remember your grandmother, Sylvara, standing at the edge of the Great Root — an oak so ancient its trunk was wider than most human buildings. She had her hand pressed flat against the bark, eyes closed, lips moving in the old tongue. Reading the forest's mood, she said. Like reading a letter from a friend.

That was before the surveyors came. Before the Meridian Industrial Consortium filed the land reclamation papers. Before anyone understood what clearcut meant when applied to a living ecosystem that had been breathing for ten thousand years.

choices.text: Remember the day the surveyors arrived
Remember Sylvara's teachings
Remember the other children in the canopy

welf_003.title: The Surveyors
text: They came in three aerodyne transports, Meridian corporate livery gleaming against the green. You watched from the canopy — sixty feet up, hidden in the embrace of a silverleaf elm — as they drove metal stakes into the soil and attached sensor packages that hummed with frequencies that made your teeth ache.

The lead surveyor was a human woman with augmented eyes that glowed faintly blue. She walked through the undergrowth like it was an inconvenience, pushing aside ferns that had been growing since before her great-grandmother was born. She spoke into a comm unit in clipped, efficient sentences: 'Viable. Soil composition optimal for foundation work. Recommend full clear and grade. Biomass can be processed for carbon credits.'

Carbon credits. Three hundred thousand acres of living forest, reduced to a line item on a corporate balance sheet. Your grandmother found you in the tree that evening, shaking with a rage you didn't have words for yet. She held you and said nothing, because there was nothing to say. She had lived long enough to know what was coming.

The Meridian Consortium had the legal right. The Expansion Accords of 2247 gave corporations eminent domain over any 'undeveloped natural resource zone' within five hundred miles of a megacity boundary. The elves had no legal standing. The forest had no legal standing. Only profit had standing.

choices.text: Remember the council's response
Remember trying to fight back

welf_004.title: Sylvara's Teachings
text: Your grandmother was seven hundred and twelve years old when she began teaching you the old ways. Her hands, still smooth despite the centuries, would guide yours along the bark of the Great Root, pressing your small fingers into the grooves where the Whisper Web pulsed closest to the surface.
'Feel it,' she would say in the old tongue. 'Not with your skin. With the part of you that remembers being a seed.'

She taught you the Songs of Growing — melodies that could coax a sapling to stretch toward light, or convince a wounded tree to seal its bark over a scar. She taught you the Reading, the art of pressing your consciousness into the root network and sensing the forest's vast, slow thoughts. And she taught you the Remembering — the elven practice of holding memory so precisely that a moment from three centuries ago could be relived as vividly as breakfast.

It is the Remembering that tortures you now. Human survivors forget. Their grief softens at the edges, blurs into something bearable. But an elf remembers the exact green of every leaf, the precise texture of every branch, the specific warmth of sunlight filtered through a canopy that no longer exists. You remember it all, and it is all gone, and the Remembering will not let you forget for the centuries you have left to live.

choices.text: Remember the day the surveyors arrived
Open your eyes — return to the present

welf_005.title: Children of the Canopy
text: There were eleven of you in the canopy cohort — the children born within the same decade, raised together in the treetop nurseries where the branches wove themselves into cradles at the elders' request. You learned to walk on branches before you learned to walk on ground. Falling was not a fear; the trees always caught you.

Lirien, who could climb faster than anyone and laughed like wind through leaves. Thandel, quiet and serious, who spent hours pressing his ear to the bark and claimed he could hear the trees dreaming. Maelis, who wove crowns from living vines and said she would be the first elf to grow a tree inside a human city.

Of the eleven, you know the fate of three. Lirien died in the burning — she went back into the fire to find a family of fox-spirits that nested in the eastern grove. They found her body curled around the den, her arms blackened to the bone. The foxes were already gone. Thandel survived and lives somewhere in the lower city, but he hasn't spoken since the camps. His mind went into the Whisper Web when the forest died and part of it never came back.
Maelis. You don't know about Maelis. You've looked. For eighty-seven years you've looked.

choices.text: Remember the day everything burned
Open your eyes — Maelis is a wound that won't heal

welf_006.title: The Carved Token
text: Your fingers find the token beneath the flat pillow — a disc of heartwood from the Great Root, carved with the sigil of your family line. Sylvara pressed it into your hand the day the evacuation order came, her ancient fingers trembling for the first time in your memory.
'This wood still breathes,' she told you. 'As long as it breathes, we are not extinct. We are not a memory. We are a people in exile, and exile ends.'

You hold it now against your chest. The wood is warm. It has always been warm, even in the coldest winters of the refugee camps, even in the concrete tomb of your Sector 7 room. Eighty-seven years, and it still carries heat, as though somewhere deep in its grain a fragment of the Verdant Reaches still lives.

You press it to your ear, an old habit, a child's habit. And for a moment — just a moment — you think you hear it. A pulse. Slow, deep, impossibly far away. The heartbeat of a forest that died before you finished growing up.

choices.text: Let the memory take you back
Pocket the token and face the day

welf_007.title: The Elder Council
text: The Council of Roots met in the hollow beneath the Great Root — a natural amphitheater where the tree's massive roots arched overhead like the ribs of a cathedral. Seventeen elders, the youngest four hundred years old, sat in a circle on seats grown from living wood. Sylvara spoke for the traditionalists.

'We have treaties,' she said, her voice steady as bedrock. 'The Accord of Living Spaces, signed in 2089, guarantees sovereign rights to any species demonstrating continuous habitation of a biome for more than five hundred years. We have been here for ten thousand.'

But Elder Coravel, the pragmatist, had already consulted human lawyers. The Accord had been superseded by the Expansion Acts. The treaties were toilet paper. The corporations had rewritten the law while the elves were busy living in harmony with nature, and the new law said that harmony was an inefficient use of resources.

The council argued for eleven days. By the time they reached consensus — a formal protest to the Meridian board, a legal challenge in human courts, an appeal to the United Species Commission — the first bulldozers had already arrived.

choices.text: Remember the day the machines came
Remember trying to fight

welf_008.title: The Resistance
text: Some of you fought. Not the elders — they believed in process, in diplomacy, in the fundamental reasonableness of beings who had already demonstrated none. But the younger elves, the ones who hadn't lived long enough to learn patience, took to the trees with everything they had.

You were among them. Forty years old, burning with a fury that felt like forest fire in your veins. You and twenty others formed the Thornguard — a guerrilla unit that sabotaged machinery, collapsed access roads, and used the old nature-songs to accelerate vine growth across the logging paths. For three weeks, you held them at the eastern perimeter.

Then Meridian sent the military contractors. Private security with thermal optics and incendiary rounds. They didn't negotiate. They didn't warn. They fired into the canopy and watched the burning elves fall like leaves.

You survived because Sylvara pulled you out. She found you in the smoke, half-blind, screaming the War Songs that hadn't been sung in a thousand years, and she dragged you bodily from the tree line. 'Dying is easy,' she hissed in your ear. 'Living is the resistance now.'

choices.text: Remember the final burning
Open your eyes — the rage is still fresh

welf_009.title: The Burning
text: They didn't cut the forest down. Cutting was too slow, too expensive. Meridian's efficiency experts calculated that controlled burning followed by chemical soil treatment was forty-three percent more cost-effective than traditional logging. So they burned it.

You stood on a ridge two miles east and watched three hundred thousand acres of ten-thousand-year-old forest become a wall of fire that stretched from horizon to horizon. The heat reached you even at that distance — a physical pressure against your skin, like the hand of a god pressing you backward. The smoke column rose miles into the atmosphere and turned the sun the color of blood.

Through the Whisper Web — the root network that connected every tree to every other — you felt them dying. Not as metaphor. Not as poetry. As sensation. Every tree that burned sent a pulse of agony through the network, and every elf with roots in that network felt it. Elders collapsed. Children screamed. Sylvara stood rigid, her jaw locked, tears carving channels through the ash on her face, and she did not look away. She bore witness. She made herself remember every second.

The fire burned for nine days. When it was over, the Verdant Reaches was a plain of ash and blackened stumps stretching to the horizon. The Whisper Web was silent for the first time in ten millennia. The silence was the worst part. The silence was an amputation.

choices.text: Remember the refugee camps
You can't bear any more — open your eyes

welf_010.title: The Present
text: Your eyes open to rust-stained concrete and the buzz of a failing light strip. The present. The only tense that matters when the past is a graveyard and the future is a debt you can't pay.

Sector 7. The Lower District. The bottom rung of a city that was built, in part, on the lumber and processed biomass of your ancestral home. You've calculated it once, in a fit of masochistic arithmetic: approximately twelve percent of the structural material in the city's lower forty floors came from Verdant Reaches timber. You live inside the corpse of your forest.

The room is small, gray, and smells of rust. A cot, a shelf, a cracked mirror. On the shelf: a battered datapad, a handful of credit chips, and a knife with tape around the handle. The sum total of a life that has lasted a hundred and twenty-seven years and accumulated nothing but grief.
Your datapad buzzes. A message. No sender ID, just a location pin: an address in the Market District, and three words in the old tongue. Words you haven't seen written in decades.

choices.text: Read the old-tongue message
Ignore it — you've chased ghosts before
Check the cracked mirror first

welf_011.title: The Camps
text: The United Species Commission set up the refugee processing center in an abandoned industrial park on the city's eastern fringe. Concrete buildings with corrugated metal roofs, designed for storing machine parts, now storing people. They called it Temporary Relocation Facility 7. Everyone called it the Cage.

Four thousand elves, crammed into a space built for inventory. The air recyclers couldn't handle the load — the air tasted of bodies and despair and the chemical tang of grief suppressants the aid workers distributed like candy. Take one, feel less. Take two, feel nothing. Take three and you might sleep through the night without dreaming of fire.

Sylvara refused the suppressants. She sat in the corner of your shared cell — twelve by twelve feet, meant for a single human, holding four elves — and she Remembered. Deliberately, methodically, she went through every day she had lived in the forest, cataloging it in the precise detail that only an elven mind could sustain. 'If we forget,' she told you, 'they win completely. The trees are dead. The memory must live.'

You spent three years in the Cage. When they finally processed your asylum claim and assigned you a housing unit in Sector 7, you walked into your concrete room, looked at the concrete walls, lay down on the concrete floor, and wept until your body ran out of water.

choices.text: Remember learning to survive in the city
Remember Sylvara's final teaching
Open your eyes — return to the present

welf_012.title: Words in the Old Tongue
text: The words glow on your datapad's cracked screen: 'Thar'sylien vae morath.' In the old tongue, it means 'The roots remember the way.' It's a phrase from the Greenwalking ceremonies — the rite of passage where young elves navigated the forest using only the Whisper Web's guidance, letting the root network guide their steps through miles of trackless woodland.

Nobody uses the old tongue anymore. Not in casual communication. The younger elves born in the city barely know it. Whoever sent this message is old enough to remember the forest, literate enough to write in a script that was traditionally only carved into living wood, and careful enough to strip their sender identification.

The location pin points to a tea house in the Market District called the Jade Lantern. You've passed it before — one of the few establishments in the lower city that serves actual plant-derived tea instead of the synthetic flavored water that passes for refreshment down here. The owner is human. You've never been inside.
Three words in a dead language. A pin drop in neutral territory. Either this is a trap, or it's the first interesting thing that's happened to you in eleven years.

choices.text: Go to the Jade Lantern
Do some research first — who owns the Jade Lantern?
Bring the knife, just in case

welf_013.title: The Cynic's Morning
text: You toss the datapad back on the shelf. Messages in the old tongue. Location pins from anonymous senders. You've been down this road. Seven years ago, a message in old tongue led you to a warehouse in the Scrap Yards where a human con artist had set up a fake elven cultural preservation society. Entrance fee: two hundred credits. Offered services: communal Remembering sessions and root tea ceremonies using synthetic substitutes. The tea was flavored water. The Remembering was a human therapist reading from a script.
You broke his arm. It wasn't your finest moment, but it was honest.

Instead of chasing phantoms, you pull on your boots — synthetic leather, nothing living, nothing real — and face the routine that has kept you alive for eighty-seven years in this concrete desert. Work. Eat. Sleep. Don't feel. Repeat.
But as you reach for the door handle, the datapad buzzes again. Same message. Same pin. And this time, a postscript in the old tongue: 'Maelis lives.'

choices.text: Grab the datapad — Maelis?
It's bait. It has to be bait. Leave.

welf_014.title: The Reflection
text: The cracked mirror shows a face that doesn't match the one in your memories. The angular cheekbones are still there, the elongated ears, the eyes that hold light in ways that make humans stare. But the vitality is gone. Wood elf skin should carry a faint luminescence — a bioluminescent undertone inherited from millennia of symbiosis with phosphorescent fungi in the deep forest. Your skin is gray. The luminescence faded around year thirty in the city, when your body finally accepted that there was no forest left to synchronize with.

Your hair, once the deep auburn of autumn canopy, has thinned and dulled to a colorless brown. You look, if you're honest with yourself, like a houseplant that someone forgot to water. Still alive, technically. But not growing. Not thriving. Just persisting, out of biological stubbornness.

A hundred and twenty-seven years old. In the forest, you'd still be considered young. A hundred and twenty-seven was the age when elves traditionally began their First Wandering — a decade-long journey through the deep reaches to find their life's purpose. Your First Wandering led you to a twelve-by-twelve concrete box and a job sorting recycled plastics at a processing plant in the Foundry district.
The datapad buzzes again. The message is waiting.

choices.text: Read the message
Ignore it and head to work

welf_015.title: City Lessons
text: The city taught you its own kind of survival. Not the patient, cyclical survival of the forest — the desperate, angular survival of concrete and competition. In the forest, resources renewed. In the city, everything was finite and everything had a price.

You learned to navigate the Lower District's informal economy. Sorting recyclables at the Foundry for twelve credits an hour. Running messages through the underground tunnels for couriers who valued your elven night vision and light step. Occasionally, when credits ran short, selling small displays of the old nature-songs at market stalls — coaxing a flower to bloom from a crack in the concrete, making a dead branch sprout a single green leaf. Parlor tricks. The last embers of a power that once moved forests.
Humans watched with the same expression they wore at the zoo: fascinated, briefly, and then moving on to the next spectacle. They tipped in small coins and smaller attention spans.

You learned to eat synthetic food without gagging. You learned to breathe recycled air without screaming. You learned to sleep on surfaces that had never been alive. You adapted. And every adaptation felt like a small death, a voluntary amputation of something that had once made you whole.

choices.text: Open your eyes — return to the present

welf_016.title: Sylvara's Last Teaching
text: Sylvara died forty-one years ago, in the autumn. She was seven hundred and fifty-three. For a wood elf, that was middle-aged — she should have had centuries left. But the Hollowing took her, as it took so many of the elders. The disconnect from the Whisper Web, from the forest, from the living world that had sustained them for millennia, created a void that their bodies eventually refused to survive.

She spent her last month teaching you. Not the nature-songs or the Remembering — those you already knew. She taught you something harder. She taught you how to carry a dead world inside you without letting it crush you.

'The forest lives in you,' she said, her voice thin as paper. 'Not as metaphor. As fact. Every elf who remembers carries a fragment of the Whisper Web in their nervous system. We are the seeds. The concrete cannot last forever. Nothing built by short-lived species can. When it crumbles — and it will crumble — we must be ready to plant.'

She pressed the heartwood token into your hand, closed your fingers around it, and died between one breath and the next. Seven hundred and fifty-three years of memory, extinguished in a concrete room that smelled of antiseptic. You carved her name into the wall with your fingernails and did not speak for a month.

choices.text: Open your eyes — carry her teaching forward

welf_017.title: The Jade Lantern
text: The Market District is three levels up from Sector 7 — a marginal improvement measured in slightly cleaner air and slightly less aggressive patrol drone coverage. The streets here are wider, lined with vendor stalls selling everything from bootleg neural patches to synthetic meat on sticks. Holographic signs compete for attention in a dozen languages, their light painting your skin in shifting colors as you walk.

The Jade Lantern sits at the end of a narrow side street, its entrance marked by a single green light that glows with a warmth that makes your chest tighten. Green. Actual green, not the synthetic approximation the city uses for traffic signals. Whoever chose that light knew what it would mean to an elf.

Inside, the air changes. It takes you a full three seconds to identify what's different, and when you do, your knees nearly buckle. The air smells of growing things. Not synthetic plant-scent, not the chemical approximation pumped into upper-level shopping arcades. Actual chlorophyll. Something alive is growing in this building.
A human woman behind the counter nods at you and gestures toward a door at the back. 'You're expected,' she says. 'Downstairs.'

choices.text: Go downstairs
Ask the woman who's expecting you
Check the room for threats first

welf_018.title: Research
text: You pull up what you can on the Jade Lantern through the public data networks. The registered owner is one Hana Voss, human, age fifty-two. Business license filed eleven years ago. Tax records clean. No flags in the Meridian security database, which either means she's legitimate or she's very good at hiding.

You dig deeper, using search skills honed by years of looking for scattered elves across the city's data networks. The Jade Lantern has an unusual supply chain — it imports actual plant matter from hydroponic farms in the mid-levels. Expensive. The tea house doesn't charge enough to cover those costs, which means either Hana Voss is running the business at a loss, or she has another income stream.

One more detail catches your eye: the building the Jade Lantern occupies has a sub-basement that doesn't appear on the city planning database. A void in the blueprints. Someone has been very carefully editing the public records to hide whatever's underneath that tea house.
Interesting. And troubling.

choices.text: Go to the Jade Lantern, but carefully
This is suspicious — prepare for trouble

welf_019.title: Armed and Wary
text: You take the knife from the shelf, check the edge — still sharp, despite the deteriorating handle — and tuck it into your belt beneath your jacket. Old habits. In the forest, you never carried weapons; the forest itself was your protection. In the city, trust is a luxury and steel is a necessity.

The walk to the Market District takes twenty minutes through corridors and connector bridges that span the gaps between hab-blocks. You keep your ears sharp — elven hearing picks up frequencies humans miss, and more than once it's saved you from muggers or patrol sweeps. Today the background noise is normal: the hum of generators, the drip of condensation, distant music from a bar that never closes.

You arrive at the Jade Lantern with one hand near your knife and every sense extended. The green light above the door stops you. It's not an LED. It's bioluminescence. Someone has cultivated a living light source — a moss or lichen — and mounted it in the fixture. Your hand drops from your knife. No one who grows living light in this dead city means you harm.
Or that's what they want you to think.

choices.text: Enter the tea house
Scout the perimeter first

welf_020.title: The Name That Burns
text: Your hand shakes as you pick up the datapad. The words are there, glowing on the cracked screen: 'Maelis lives.'

Maelis. The girl who wove crowns from living vines. The girl who swore she'd grow a tree inside a city. Your closest friend in the canopy cohort, the one whose laughter sounded like wind through leaves, the one who disappeared during the evacuation and never surfaced in any refugee database, any casualty list, any record of any kind. Just gone. A name without a fate.

For eighty-seven years, you've searched. You've queried every database you could access, bribed information brokers, visited every elven community in the lower and middle city. Nothing. The prevailing theory was that she died in the burning — her body lost in the ash like so many others, unidentified, uncounted, unmourned except by those who remembered her face.

If this is a lie, if someone is using her name to lure you, you will find them and you will make them regret learning the old tongue. If it's true — if Maelis is alive — then eighty-seven years of grief just shifted on its foundations, and you don't know whether what you feel is hope or terror.

choices.text: Go to the Jade Lantern immediately
Research the message first — trust nothing

welf_021.title: Walking Away
text: You leave the datapad on the shelf and walk out the door. The corridor is the same gray it was yesterday and the day before and the eighty-seven years of days before that. You take the stairs down — the elevator hasn't worked since the power redistribution cut capacity to the lower floors — and push through the lobby door into the street.

Acid rain mists down from the overhead levels. You pull your hood up and start walking toward the transit hub. Shift starts at the Foundry in forty minutes. Twelve hours of sorting recyclables, then back to the room, then sleep, then repeat. The machine grinds on.

You make it two blocks before you stop. Your feet stop. You don't decide to stop — your body makes the decision for you, planting itself on the wet concrete like a tree putting down roots. You stand in the rain and you think about Maelis and you think about Sylvara saying 'we are the seeds' and you think about eighty-seven years of nothing and you think about a message in the old tongue from someone who knows things only a forest elf would know.
You turn around.

choices.text: Go back for the datapad
Go straight to the Jade Lantern — you remember the address

welf_022.title: Beneath the Lantern
text: The stairs descend two flights into a sub-basement that the city records say doesn't exist. The air grows warmer with each step. Moister. And the smell of growing things intensifies until you have to stop and press your hand against the wall because your body is responding to stimuli it hasn't encountered in decades. Your skin prickles. The dormant bioluminescence beneath your dermis flickers — a faint, impossible glow that you haven't seen since the first year in the camps.
The basement opens into a room that stops your heart.

Trees. Small — saplings, really, none taller than six feet — but alive, growing from soil beds carved into the concrete floor and illuminated by full-spectrum grow lights. Ferns cascade from wall-mounted planters. Moss covers every horizontal surface in a carpet of impossible green. And in the center of the room, in a ceramic pot that seems too small for its importance, stands a seedling with leaves you recognize. Silver-edged. Faintly luminescent. The unmistakable profile of a Verdant Reaches silverleaf elm.

You can't breathe. You can't breathe because the last silverleaf elm died eighty-seven years ago in a fire that consumed your entire world, and yet here one stands, small and defiant and alive, in a basement beneath a tea house in the most hostile city on earth.

choices.text: Touch the silverleaf seedling
Look for whoever brought you here
Weep

welf_023.title: The Tea House Owner
text: The woman studies you with eyes that are sharp and unhurried. 'You're the last one,' she says. 'She said you'd come, but you'd come slow. Said you were stubborn.'
'She?' The word comes out harder than you intend. 'Who is she?'

The woman smiles in a way that suggests she's had this conversation before, perhaps many times, with other suspicious elves. 'I'm Hana. I've run this shop for eleven years. Before that, I ran a botanical research lab for Meridian, until I discovered what they'd done to the Verdant Reaches samples. I quit. Started this place. She found me three years later.'
'Who found you?' you repeat, and the patience in your voice is a thin shell over something that could be rage or hope or both.
'Go downstairs,' Hana says gently. 'She'll explain it better than I can. I'm just the one who provides cover and grows the tea.'

choices.text: Go downstairs
Demand more answers first

welf_024.title: Scouting the Perimeter
text: Old Thornguard instincts surface like muscle memory. You circle the block, mapping exits and sight lines. The Jade Lantern occupies the ground floor of a four-story building with rear access through an alley. No Meridian security cameras visible, which is unusual for the Market District — someone has either disabled them or arranged a blind spot.

The alley exit leads to a service corridor that connects to the transit hub three blocks south. Good. Multiple escape routes. The building's upper floors appear residential, lights on in two of the six windows. Normal activity.

You press your ear to the tea house's rear wall and listen. Elven hearing catches what human ears would miss: the hum of full-spectrum grow lights running at high power, the drip of an irrigation system, and — faintly, impossibly — the sound of leaves rustling in air that has no wind.

Your breath catches. Whatever is inside that building, it's alive. Actually, organically alive. In a city where the nearest living plant is in a corporate biodome sixty levels above you, someone has built a garden in the basement of a tea house.

choices.text: Enter through the front
Enter through the rear — stay tactical

welf_025.title: Prepared for Trouble
text: You arm yourself with more than the knife. From the false bottom of your shelf — a modification you made years ago — you retrieve a compact EMP charge, scavenged from a broken patrol drone and jury-rigged to function as a single-use disruptor. One press and every electronic device within fifteen feet goes dark for thirty seconds. In a city that runs on electricity, thirty seconds of darkness is an eternity.
You also take the lockpick set you assembled from old dental tools and scrap wire. Not elegant, but functional. The city teaches you to be a generalist of small crimes.

Dressed, armed, and deeply suspicious, you head for the Market District. The old Thornguard protocols run through your mind: assess, infiltrate, neutralize. You haven't used these skills in earnest since the resistance days, but they haven't dulled. Some things the body remembers even when the heart would rather forget.
If this is a trap baited with dead language and false hope, whoever set it will learn that wood elves may have lost their forest, but they have not lost their thorns.

choices.text: Approach the Jade Lantern cautiously

welf_026.title: The Silverleaf
text: Your hand moves toward the seedling as if pulled by gravity. Your fingers brush the silver-edged leaf and the world tilts.

Connection. Raw, overwhelming, immediate. The Whisper Web — or a fragment of it, a thread so thin it's barely there — pulses through the physical contact. The seedling's roots have found something in the soil beneath the concrete, some buried remnant of the ancient network, and through it you feel a ghost of what you lost. The slow, vast consciousness of living wood. The patient intelligence of a being that measures time in centuries and growth in millimeters.

The seedling knows you. Not personally — it's too young for that, barely a decade old. But it recognizes what you are. Elf. Forest-kin. Root-bound. And in its simple, vegetable way, it reaches back. A warmth spreads from your fingertips up your arm and into your chest, and the dormant bioluminescence in your skin blazes to life for the first time in eighty-seven years.

You glow. Faintly, softly, the way you used to glow in the deep forest when the phosphorescent fungi matched your heartbeat. You glow, and you weep, and someone behind you says, in a voice you haven't heard since you were forty years old: 'I told you I'd grow a tree inside a city.'

choices.text: Turn around

welf_027.title: Searching the Basement
text: You tear your eyes from the silverleaf seedling and scan the room. Beyond the central growing area, the basement extends into what appears to be a network of connected chambers. Shelves line the walls, holding jars of seeds labeled in the old tongue. Soil samples. Water filtration equipment. A workbench covered in horticultural tools — some modern, some carved from wood in styles you recognize from the forest.

This is not a hobby garden. This is a preservation operation. Systematic, organized, and clearly the work of someone with deep knowledge of Verdant Reaches botany. The seed collection alone represents dozens of species you thought were extinct.

A sound from the far chamber. Footsteps — light, almost inaudible, the kind of footfall that only an elf makes on instinct. You tense. The footsteps approach, and a figure emerges from the shadows of the far doorway.

She is older than you remember. Of course she is — eighty-seven years will do that, even to an elf. But the shape of her face, the way she tilts her head, the impossible green of her eyes — these have not changed. Could never change. You would know her if you lived a thousand years.

choices.text: Say her name

welf_028.title: Tears in the Garden
text: You fall to your knees among the ferns and you weep. Not the quiet, controlled weeping of the camps, where grief was rationed because the walls were thin and everyone was breaking. This is the other kind. The kind that comes from a place below language, below thought, from the root system of your being where the Hollowing has lived for eighty-seven years like a parasite feeding on absence.

You weep for the Verdant Reaches. For the Great Root. For Sylvara and Lirien and Thandel and all the others. For the Whisper Web and the canopy nurseries and the fox-spirits and the ten thousand years of accumulated life that was burned to nothing so a corporation could file carbon credits. You weep for yourself — for the child who walked on branches before ground, for the young fighter who screamed War Songs into the smoke, for the refugee who lay on a concrete floor and ran dry.

The moss beneath your knees is soft. Real. Alive. It absorbs your tears without comment, and somewhere in the green silence of this impossible basement garden, a seedling shivers as though touched by a breeze that doesn't exist.
A hand, warm and calloused and unmistakably elven, settles on your shoulder. A voice says your name in the old tongue, and the accent is one you haven't heard in eighty-seven years.

choices.text: Look up

welf_029.title: Demanding Answers
text: You plant your feet and fix Hana with a stare that has three centuries of genetic authority behind it. 'I've survived this long by not walking into basements on the word of strangers. Talk.'

Hana sighs and pours a cup of tea. Real tea — you can smell the tannins, the living chemistry. She pushes it across the counter toward you. 'Three years ago, an elf came to me. She'd heard about my work at the botanical lab. She knew I'd quit because of what I found in the Verdant Reaches files — the fact that Meridian had saved seed samples before the burn. Thousands of them. Stored in a corporate vault and forgotten. She asked me to help her steal them.'
Your hand tightens on the counter. 'Steal seed samples.'

'Seed samples, soil cultures, mycorrhizal networks in cryogenic suspension. Everything you'd need to regrow a forest, if you had the space, the time, and the knowledge.' Hana meets your eyes steadily. 'She has the knowledge. I provide the cover and the resources. What she needs now is people. Elves who remember the old ways. Elves who can sing the growing songs.'
The tea steams between you. It smells like something you lost a long time ago.

choices.text: Go downstairs
Drink the tea first — you need a moment

welf_030.title: The Back Way In
text: The rear door's lock is mechanical — unusual in a district where everything runs on electronic security. Your lockpick set makes short work of it. Thornguard training: any door, any lock, sixty seconds or less. You're inside in forty.

A service corridor leads past a storage room stacked with tea crates and into a narrow stairwell descending below street level. The grow lights humming below cast a green glow up the stairs that makes the concrete look alive. You descend silently, knife in hand, and emerge into the basement from the back.

The garden hits you like a physical blow. You stand in the doorway, knife forgotten in your hand, and stare at the impossible green. The saplings. The ferns. The moss. And there, in the center, the silverleaf seedling that should not exist.

A voice behind you — from the shadows of a side chamber where you failed to check — says, 'You always did prefer the back door. Even in the canopy, you'd climb the trunk instead of taking the vine ladder.'

choices.text: Spin around, knife ready
Lower the knife — you know that voice

welf_031.title: Maelis
text: Maelis stands before you, and the years have carved her the way wind carves a mountain — changing the surface while leaving the essential shape intact. Her face is leaner, the youthful softness replaced by angles that speak of decades of hard living. A scar traces the line of her left jaw, thin and silver. Her hair, once black as deep forest shadow, is threaded with premature gray at the temples. But her eyes are the same. Green as new growth, and blazing with a light that the city has never managed to extinguish.

She wears practical clothes — work boots, cargo pants, a jacket with more pockets than fabric. Her hands are calloused and stained with soil. Real soil, dark and rich and alive with the organisms that make growing things possible.

'I looked for you,' she says. 'For years, I looked. But I had to build this first. I had to build it before I could face any of you, because I needed to bring you something more than grief. I needed to bring you proof that it wasn't over.'
She gestures at the garden around her — the impossible basement forest, the stolen seeds, the silverleaf seedling growing from the ashes of extinction.
'It's not over,' she says. 'Not yet.'

choices.text: Embrace her — words can wait
"Where have you been for eighty-seven years?"
"Show me everything"

welf_032.title: The Tea
text: You pick up the cup and drink. The tea is unlike anything you've tasted in the city. It has depth, complexity, the layered flavor profile that only comes from leaves grown in real soil under real light. There are notes of mint and something floral you can't identify, and underneath it all, a faint bitterness that tastes like memory.

Your hands stop shaking. The warmth spreads through your chest and settles in the space where the Hollowing lives, not filling it — nothing can fill it — but softening its edges. For a moment, just a moment, the concrete walls don't press so close.

'She grows the tea herself,' Hana says, watching your face. 'In the basement. Along with about forty other species that the official record says are extinct. She's been at it for over a decade. Alone, mostly. But now she needs help. The project is bigger than one person.'
You set the cup down carefully. Your reflection wobbles in the dark surface of the tea, and for a second — a trick of the light, a hallucination — your skin carries a faint luminescent glow.

choices.text: Go downstairs

welf_033.title: Knife Edge
text: You spin, blade up, body dropping into the low fighting stance the Thornguard drilled into you until it became reflex. The figure in the doorway doesn't flinch. She stands with her hands visible, palms open, and she's smiling — a sad, patient, exhausted smile that holds eighty-seven years of its own pain.
'Still sharp,' she says. 'Good. You'll need to be.'

The knife lowers slowly, not because you've decided to lower it, but because your hand recognizes her before your mind does. The shape of her stance. The tilt of her head. The way she holds her weight on her left foot, the same slight imbalance she's had since she fell from the canopy at age thirty-five and cracked her ankle on a root that should have caught her.
'Maelis,' you say, and the name feels like a seed breaking through concrete.

choices.text: Lower the knife completely

welf_034.title: Reunion
text: You cross the space between you in two strides and pull her into an embrace that has eighty-seven years of momentum behind it. She is solid and warm and real — not a memory, not a ghost, not a name on a datapad screen — and she smells of soil and green things and the sharp mineral tang of underground water.

She holds you back with equal force, and for a long time neither of you speaks. Words are for people who haven't lost a world together. Words are for people whose grief can be contained in sentences. What passes between you is older than language — a shared knowledge, transmitted through touch, that says: we survived. Against all logic, against all probability, we survived, and we are standing in a room full of living things, and the story is not over.
When you finally pull apart, her eyes are wet. 'I'm sorry it took so long,' she says. 'I needed to have something to offer besides sadness.'
She gestures at the garden, at the shelves of seeds, at the silverleaf seedling glowing gently in its pot. 'I have a plan. It's dangerous and possibly insane. But it's a plan. Will you hear it?'

choices.text: "Tell me everything"

welf_035.title: Eighty-Seven Years
text: Maelis sits on a low bench carved from reclaimed wood — you can see the old nail holes where it was once part of a shipping crate — and tells her story in the measured, precise cadence of an elf practicing the Remembering. Every detail preserved. Every year accounted for.

She didn't flee with the other refugees. During the evacuation, she broke away and went back — not to fight, not to die, but to save what she could. She filled a pack with seed pods, soil samples, fragments of bark from the oldest trees. She harvested a cutting from the Great Root itself, wrapping it in wet moss and strapping it to her body like a child.

'I hid in the human districts for twenty years. Moved constantly. Worked in hydroponics labs, botanical gardens, anywhere I could learn the science to complement the old songs. I stole equipment piece by piece. Seeds from Meridian's vault — they had thousands of samples, just sitting in storage. They'd burned a ten-thousand-year-old forest and filed the seeds under Assets: Biological.'

Her voice is steady, but her hands shake. 'I've been growing things in basements and abandoned tunnels for six decades. Failing. Trying again. The silverleaf took me forty years to germinate. Forty years, for one seedling. But it grew.'

choices.text: "What's your plan?"
"I thought you were dead"

welf_036.title: The Collection
text: Maelis leads you through the connected chambers, and each room is a revelation. The first holds the seed vault — hundreds of jars, carefully labeled in the old tongue, containing seeds from seventy-three species of Verdant Reaches flora. You read the labels with a rising sense of vertigo: silverleaf elm, ironwood oak, moonvine, ghost orchid, foxfire moss, dreamwillow, thornberry, sunfern. Names you haven't spoken aloud in decades. Names you thought belonged only to the dead.

The second chamber holds the soil cultures — carefully maintained ecosystems in glass-walled terrariums, each one a micro-biome containing the fungal networks, bacterial colonies, and microorganisms that made the forest floor alive. Without these, the seeds are just potential. With them, the seeds are a forest waiting to happen.

The third chamber makes you stop breathing. A wall of cuttings in hydroponic tubes, each one growing slowly under calibrated light. And there, in the largest tube, a cutting from the Great Root itself. You can feel it — even through the glass, even through the years of separation — the faint pulse of the Whisper Web, impossibly persistent, like a heartbeat that refuses to stop.
'This is what I've been doing for eighty-seven years,' Maelis says quietly. 'Building an ark.'

choices.text: "What do you need from me?"

welf_037.title: The Plan
text: Maelis spreads a hand-drawn map on the workbench. The map shows a network of tunnels beneath the city — not the official transit tunnels, but older passages, pre-dating the megacity. Storm drains, abandoned infrastructure, natural cave systems that existed before the city was built on top of them.

'The underground network extends for hundreds of miles,' she says, tracing routes with her soil-stained finger. 'Some of these tunnels are deep enough that the city above doesn't know they exist. And in the deepest sections, there's natural water, natural soil, even some residual light from geothermal vents.'

She looks at you. 'I want to grow the forest underground. Not the whole forest — not yet. But enough to establish a root network. A new Whisper Web. If we can get the Great Root cutting to take hold and spread, if we can connect enough saplings through a new network, we can create a living system that the city can't easily destroy because it won't know it exists.'

'It's a fifty-year project. Minimum. I need elves who know the old songs to help the growing. I need people I trust to keep the secret. And I need someone to go above — to work in the city, build connections, find resources — while I stay below and tend the roots. That's where you come in.'

choices.text: "I'll do it. Whatever you need."
"This is insane. The city will find you."
"What about the other elves? Thandel?"

welf_038.title: The Weight of Absence
text: 'I know,' Maelis says, and her voice cracks for the first time. 'I know you searched. Hana helped me monitor the elf community networks — I saw your queries, your posts, your requests for information. Every one of them. For eighty-seven years, I watched you look for me and I couldn't answer.'
'Why?' The word is a blade.

'Because if anyone found me before I was ready, they'd find the seeds. Meridian tracks its biological assets. If they discovered I'd stolen from their vault, they wouldn't just come for me. They'd come for everything I'd built. And I couldn't — I could not — risk the last silverleaf seedling on earth for the sake of my own loneliness.'

She meets your eyes, and you see the cost of that choice written in every line of her face. Eighty-seven years of solitude, of working alone in basements and tunnels, of watching the elf community grieve and fragment and dissolve while she held the secret that could have given them hope. She chose the seeds over her people. She chose the future over the present. It was the hardest decision anyone has ever made in your presence, and she made it alone.
'I'm sorry,' she says. 'I'm sorry for every year.'

choices.text: Forgive her — the seeds matter more than pain
"Don't apologize. You saved us."

welf_039.title: The Oath
text: The words come out of you with a certainty that surprises you. Not the desperate certainty of the Thornguard days, when conviction was fueled by rage and adrenaline. This is something deeper. Quieter. The certainty of a seed that knows which way is up.

Maelis nods slowly. 'It won't be easy. You'll need to build a life on the surface — connections, resources, a cover identity that can withstand scrutiny. The city's power structures are complex. You'll need to navigate them. I need you to become someone in the upper world so you can protect what we're growing in the lower one.'

She pulls a small package from beneath the workbench. Inside: a new datapad, clean, untraceable. A credit chip with a modest but useful balance. And a small glass vial containing a single seed, silver-edged, glowing faintly with bioluminescence.
'A silverleaf seed,' she says. 'Keep it with you. If anything happens to this place, if everything burns again, that seed is the last chance. Guard it with the centuries you have left.'

choices.text: Choose your path: become a guardian of the growing
Choose your path: become a voice for the voiceless
Choose your path: become a shadow in the city

welf_040.title: The Realist
text: 'Maelis, listen to me.' You grip her shoulders. 'Meridian has thermal imaging, ground-penetrating sonar, drone surveillance networks that cover every square foot of this city. You can't hide a forest underground. The moment a root system gets large enough to register on their sensors, they'll find it. And they'll burn it. Again.'

She doesn't flinch. 'You think I haven't thought about that? I've spent sixty years thinking about that. The tunnels I've mapped are below the sensor floor. Pre-city infrastructure, natural cave systems. The stone composition in the deep tunnels blocks sonar. And I've developed a strain of the foxfire moss that generates electromagnetic interference — a natural jammer grown from the original Verdant Reaches stock.'

She pulls up data on a workstation screen. Sensor maps, geological surveys, patrol route analyses. This is not the dream of a grieving exile. This is a military operation planned with the precision of someone who has had decades to prepare and nothing to do but plan.
'I'm not being romantic,' she says. 'I'm being strategic. The forest won't grow in a generation. It'll take centuries. We have centuries. Humans don't. That's our advantage.'

choices.text: She's right — commit to the plan
"The plan needs more. It needs political cover."

welf_041.title: The Others
text: Maelis's face tightens. 'I've tracked as many of us as I could. Of the four thousand who came through the camps, about two thousand are still in the city. The rest scattered — other cities, the wastelands, a few who found work on the orbital stations. Of those who stayed, most have... adapted. Jobs, routines, the slow surrender of identity that the city demands as the price of survival.'

'Thandel is in Sector 4. He works in a sensory deprivation clinic — ironic, for someone whose mind is half-lost in a dead root network. He doesn't speak, but he responds to the old tongue. I've visited him. He knew me.'

She pauses. 'Lirien is dead. You know that. But what you might not know is that the foxes she went back for — the fox-spirits from the eastern grove — some of them survived. A mating pair and three kits. They're here, in the deepest tunnel. They've been breeding. The fox-spirit population is up to seventeen.'
You close your eyes. Seventeen fox-spirits, alive in a tunnel beneath a city that burned their home. The scales of extinction tip, just slightly, toward survival.
'When this is ready,' Maelis says, 'I want to bring in others. But not yet. For now, the circle stays small.'

choices.text: "I'm in. Tell me what you need."
"We should bring Thandel in. He needs this."

welf_042.title: The Guardian Path
text: 'I was Thornguard once,' you say. 'I defended the forest with blade and song. The forest is different now — smaller, fragile, hidden — but the duty is the same. I'll be the guardian.'

Maelis nods. 'Then you'll need to be stronger than you are. The city's threats aren't trees you can climb or animals you can speak to. Corporate security, gang territories, corrupt officials — the dangers here wear suits and carry weapons that make elven bows look like toys. You need to learn to fight in their world.'

She hands you a name and an address. 'Kael. Ex-military, discharged under questionable circumstances. He runs a combat training gym in the Foundry district. Teaches anyone who can pay, asks no questions. Tell him Hana sent you.'
'The guardian path is the hardest,' Maelis says. 'You'll be the one standing between the garden and everything that wants to destroy it. Are you sure?'
You think of Sylvara dragging you from the burning tree line. Living is the resistance now. You square your shoulders.

choices.text: Begin training as the guardian

welf_043.title: The Voice Path
text: 'The forest doesn't just need protection,' you say. 'It needs a voice. Someone to work the political channels, build alliances, make connections with other displaced communities. If we stay hidden forever, we stay vulnerable forever. Eventually, we need allies.'
Maelis looks at you with something that might be relief. 'I was hoping you'd say that. I'm a gardener, not a diplomat. The political landscape of this city is a jungle I don't know how to navigate.'

She gives you a different packet. Contacts in the Market District — community organizers, low-level political operatives, journalists who cover non-human rights. 'Start small,' she says. 'Build trust. Find out who in this city's power structure would shelter a secret forest, and who would sell us out for a Meridian bonus.'
'The voice path is the most exposed,' she warns. 'You'll be visible. Known. That's dangerous. But it's also necessary. We can't grow in silence forever.'
You think of the Elder Council, arguing for eleven days while the bulldozers arrived. This time, the voice will be faster. And it won't ask permission.

choices.text: Begin building connections

welf_044.title: The Shadow Path
text: 'I know how to move unseen,' you say. 'The city has shadows, and I've been living in them for eighty-seven years. Let me be the one who works in the dark. Procurement, information, sabotage if necessary. The things that need doing but can't be done in daylight.'
Maelis regards you with a steady gaze. 'That's the path I've been walking. It's lonely. The weight of secrets compounds over decades.'
'I know.'

She nods slowly. 'Then you'll need better tools than a knife with a taped handle. There's a fixer in the underground tunnels — goes by the name Switch. She supplies equipment to people who don't officially exist. Tell her the old-tongue passphrase: Var'eth nomine. She'll know what it means.'

'The shadow path is the most dangerous,' Maelis says. 'If you're caught, there's no political cover, no community protection. You'll be alone, and the city's justice system doesn't distinguish between espionage and terrorism. But the things you can learn in the dark — corporate secrets, security vulnerabilities, the locations of other seed vaults — those could be the difference between survival and extinction.'

choices.text: Begin walking the shadow path

welf_045.title: The Political Angle
text: 'You're right about the sensors, about the geology, about the timeline,' you say. 'But you're missing a layer. What happens when the forest is too big to hide? What happens when roots start cracking foundations and saplings push through basement floors? You need political infrastructure. Allies in the city government, sympathizers in the corporate structure, a legal framework that protects what you've built.'
Maelis stares at you. 'I've been underground too long. I think in terms of soil and roots. You're right — we need people above too.'

'Let me work the surface,' you say. 'Build connections. Find out who in this city might actually give a damn about a dead forest and the people who lost it. Not everyone above is Meridian. There have to be allies.'
She's quiet for a long time. Then: 'Sylvara always said you thought in systems. She said you saw the whole tree where the rest of us saw only branches.'
The mention of Sylvara's name in this underground garden, among these impossible survivors, feels like a benediction.

choices.text: Commit to building the political cover
"I'll do both — surface and shadow"

welf_046.title: Thandel's Name
text: 'Thandel,' you say. 'He's half-lost in the dead Web. But if there's a living fragment here — if the Great Root cutting is generating even a whisper of network activity — maybe it could reach him. Maybe it could bring him back.'

Maelis's eyes widen. 'I hadn't thought of that. The cutting does generate network pulses — I've measured them. They're weak, barely detectable, but they're real. If Thandel's mind is still partially interfaced with the old Web, a living pulse might...'

She trails off, then moves quickly to the workstation, pulling up readings from sensors embedded in the Great Root cutting's hydroponic tube. Numbers scroll across the screen — electromagnetic frequencies, biochemical markers, pulse patterns. To a human, it would be meaningless data. To an elf who was taught by Sylvara, it reads like a heartbeat.

'It's possible,' she says quietly. 'Bringing Thandel here, letting him interface with the cutting — it's possible it could reconnect the fractured pathways. But it's also possible it could break him completely. The Web he's holding onto is dead. Showing him a living fragment might be healing. Or it might be cruelty.'
'He deserves the chance,' you say.

choices.text: "I'll bring him. After I've established myself above."

welf_047.title: The Foundry Training
text: Kael's gym occupies a converted machine shop in the Foundry district. The walls still carry the scars of industrial equipment — bolt holes, weld marks, the ghost outlines of machines long since scrapped. Now the space holds training mats, weapons racks, and a fight ring stained with sweat and occasional blood.

Kael himself is a mountain of scarred muscle — human, ex-corporate military, with the thousand-yard stare of someone who's seen things that don't fit in official reports. He looks you up and down when you arrive. 'Hana sent you,' he says. Statement, not question. 'You're the elf.'

He doesn't waste time with pleasantries. The first day, he beats you. Thoroughly, efficiently, without malice. He maps your abilities with the precision of a combat instructor: 'Fast, flexible, good reflexes. No power. No weight. No experience with close-quarters fighting in enclosed spaces. The forest taught you to fight in three dimensions. The city fights in one.'

Over the following weeks, he reshapes your combat instincts. Corridor fighting. Room clearing. Knife work in spaces too small to swing. Using your elven agility in concrete environments — wall runs, ceiling braces, the vertical movement that most city fighters don't expect. You learn to fight dirty, because clean fighting is a luxury of people who aren't protecting the last forest on earth.

choices.text: Continue the guardian's training

welf_048.title: First Connections
text: The Market District's political ecosystem is a web of competing interests held together by favors and fear. You start where Maelis's contacts point you: a community organizer named Dara Senn who runs a non-human rights advocacy group out of a converted storage unit.

Dara is half-orc, half-human — a combination that gives her exactly zero allies in the pure-race political blocs and a lifetime of experience navigating hostility from every direction. She listens to your carefully edited pitch — a wood elf interested in community organizing, looking for ways to help displaced non-humans — with the expression of someone who's heard a thousand pitches and believed three.

'Everyone wants to help,' she says. 'Nobody wants to do the work. The work is boring. The work is filing permits, attending council meetings, building case files against corporations that have legal teams bigger than our entire district. It's not heroic. There are no songs written about people who fill out forms.'
'My people's songs are all about things that take centuries,' you say. 'Patience is the one advantage we haven't lost.'
She studies you for a long moment, then slides a chair toward you. 'Can you start today?'

choices.text: Begin the work of building alliances

welf_049.title: Switch
text: The underground tunnels beneath the transit hub are a labyrinth of pre-city infrastructure — storm drains, utility conduits, abandoned subway lines from an era when public transit was still public. You navigate by elven night vision and the mental map Maelis provided, counting junctions and reading the spray-painted tags that mark the tunnel runners' territory.

Switch operates from a dead-end tunnel sealed with a blast door that was clearly salvaged from a military installation. You speak the passphrase through an intercom: 'Var'eth nomine.' The door opens with a hiss of pressurized air.

The woman inside is small, wiry, with more cybernetic modifications than organic parts. Her left eye is a camera lens. Her right arm is a multi-tool. She moves with the jerky precision of someone whose nervous system is more circuit than synapse. She looks at you with her camera eye and says, 'Elf. Don't see many of you down here.'
'You'll be seeing more,' you say.

She outfits you with a proper toolkit: a signal scrambler, a set of electronic lockpicks that make your dental-tool set look prehistoric, a subdermal comm patch, and a monofilament blade barely thicker than a hair that cuts through any material softer than diamond. 'Try not to die,' she says. 'Repeat customers are better for business.'

choices.text: Begin shadow operations

welf_050.title: Tested by Fire
text: Three months into your training, the test comes. Not from Kael — from the city. You're walking back from the gym through the Foundry's industrial corridors when you hear a sound that freezes your blood: the shriek of an alarm, followed by the unmistakable crack of a weapon discharge.

Around the corner, in a loading bay used by recycling crews, three men in unmarked gear are dragging someone toward a transport. The victim is an elf — young, maybe thirty, barely into adulthood. You don't recognize her, but you recognize the terror on her face. It's the face of someone being taken by people who don't intend to file paperwork.

Kael's training takes over. You move before you think. Wall run to gain height. Drop onto the first man from above, driving your knee into his shoulder joint. Spin, elbow strike to the second man's throat. The third raises his weapon and you close the distance in a way that no human can match — elven speed, elven reflexes, applied to the brutal close-quarters techniques Kael hammered into you.
Twenty seconds. All three down. The young elf stares at you from the floor, shaking. You pull her up and you say, in the old tongue, 'Run.' She does.

choices.text: Search the men for information
Follow the young elf — make sure she's safe

welf_051.title: The Long Game
text: Dara was right. The work is boring. You attend district council meetings where representatives argue over sewage allocation while non-human communities lack basic water filtration. You file complaints that are acknowledged and ignored. You write letters to corporate liaison offices that respond with form messages. The bureaucracy is a machine designed to grind hope into paperwork.

But you're an elf. You measure time differently. While human activists burn out in years, you settle in for decades. You build relationships with the slow patience of a tree growing around a stone. A favor here. A connection there. A quiet word in the right ear at the right time.

Six months in, you score your first real victory: a zoning exemption that allows a community garden in Sector 3. Actual soil, actual plants, in a district where green hasn't grown in fifty years. It's tiny — a twenty-by-twenty plot on a rooftop. But the day the first seedling breaks soil, you stand there with tears on your face and think: this is how it starts. One plot at a time. One root at a time.
Dara finds you staring at the garden and says, 'You're playing a longer game than I thought.' You smile and don't answer. She has no idea how long.

choices.text: Continue building political capital
Use the garden victory to recruit allies

welf_052.title: First Shadow Run
text: Maelis's first assignment is straightforward: infiltrate a Meridian subsidiary's data storage facility in the Foundry district and download their biological asset registry. The registry contains the locations of every seed vault, tissue sample, and genetic archive the corporation controls. If you can get that data, Maelis can identify which assets are unguarded enough to liberate.

The facility is a converted warehouse with standard corporate security: badge access, camera coverage, two armed guards on rotation. Child's play for someone with elven night vision, Switch's tools, and the desperate motivation of a person protecting the last seeds of an extinct world.

You go at three AM, when the guard rotation leaves a ninety-second gap in coverage. The signal scrambler kills the cameras. The electronic lockpicks open the server room. Your fingers move across the terminal with the precise speed of someone who has spent eighty-seven years learning to operate human technology, and the registry downloads in forty-five seconds.
You're out before the guards complete their next pass. Clean. Silent. The monofilament blade never leaves its sheath. You don't want to hurt anyone. You want to steal back what was stolen from you.

choices.text: Deliver the data to Maelis
Analyze the data yourself first

welf_053.title: Intelligence from Violence
text: The three men carry no identification — professional. But one of them has a comm unit with a partially encrypted message log. You pocket it. Another has a syringe loaded with a chemical compound you don't recognize, labeled only with a serial number. The third has a datapad with a list of names. Elven names.

Your blood goes cold. Twelve names, with addresses and daily schedules. The young elf who was being taken is on the list. So are six others you recognize from the refugee community. Next to each name, a status: 'PENDING,' 'ACQUIRED,' or 'PROCESSED.' Three names are marked 'PROCESSED.' You don't know what that means, but the word sits in your gut like a stone.

At the bottom of the list, a note: 'PRIORITY ASSETS — Verdant Reaches genetic markers. Meridian BioDiv requires 12 specimens for Project Thornwall. Extraction protocol: non-lethal preferred, lethal authorized.'
They're harvesting elves. Meridian is harvesting elves for their genetic material. The same corporation that burned your forest is now coming for the people who survived it.

choices.text: Take this information to Maelis immediately
Warn the elves on the list first

welf_054.title: The Rescued Elf
text: You find her two blocks away, crouched behind a steam vent, shaking so hard her teeth chatter. She's young — painfully young, maybe born in the camps, never saw the forest. Her ears mark her as wood elf, but her accent is pure lower-city slang. A child of exile.
'Hey,' you say in the old tongue, and her head snaps up. She stares at you with eyes that are the deep green of forest shade, incongruous in her city-worn face. 'You speak — are you —'
'I'm like you,' you say. 'From the camps. Before the camps, the forest.'

Her name is Sera. She's twenty-two. She works in a data entry center in Sector 5. She was walking home when the men grabbed her. She doesn't know why. She doesn't know about any list or any project or any reason why someone would want a broke elf girl from the slums.

But you know. Looking at her — at her green eyes, her elven features, her genetic heritage written in every cell — you know exactly why Meridian wants her. She carries the Verdant Reaches in her blood, and to a corporation that destroyed a forest and kept the seeds, the blood of the survivors is just another asset to harvest.

choices.text: Take Sera somewhere safe and investigate
Bring Sera to the Jade Lantern

welf_055.title: The Council Seat
text: A year of patient work earns you what no wood elf has held since arriving in the city: a seat on the Lower District Community Advisory Council. It's a toothless position — advisory only, no legislative power — but it comes with access. Access to district planning documents, security briefings, corporate development proposals. Information that Maelis needs and cannot get from underground.

Your first council briefing includes a line item that makes your pulse spike: 'Meridian BioDiv — Project Thornwall — Biological Asset Acquisition Program.' No details. The line item is buried in a hundred pages of infrastructure reports. Nobody else on the council noticed it. But you did, because you know what 'biological asset' means when a corporation uses the term about your people.

You make a copy and send it to Maelis through the secure channel Hana established. That night, Maelis messages back: 'I've heard rumors. They're collecting genetic material from Verdant Reaches survivors. For what purpose, I don't know. But it can't be good.'
The political game just got more dangerous. And more necessary.

choices.text: Investigate Project Thornwall through political channels
Focus on building protective policies for non-humans

welf_056.title: Growing the Network
text: The community garden becomes a seed of its own — not just for plants, but for trust. You use the rooftop plot as a gathering point, inviting non-human community leaders to tend the soil alongside humans. An orc mechanic named Gruul, who lost his family in the Gene Riots, comes every Thursday to water the tomatoes. A synth artist named Echo paints murals on the surrounding walls. A human doctor named Chen donates medical supplies in exchange for herbs you grow from seeds Maelis provides.

None of them know about the underground garden. None of them know about the silverleaf seedling or the Great Root cutting or the fox-spirits in the deep tunnels. But each of them, in their own way, is an ally. Each connection is a root that strengthens the network.

You're building something Sylvara would have recognized: a community. Not an elven community — a community. Species mixed, backgrounds varied, united by the simple act of making things grow in a place where growth was never intended.

When the district council tries to revoke the garden's zoning exemption six months later, forty people show up to protest. The council backs down. It's a small victory. You celebrate with a cup of Hana's real tea and a quiet moment with the silverleaf seed in your pocket.

choices.text: Continue building the network

welf_057.title: The Registry
text: Maelis receives the data like a soldier receiving intelligence from the front line — with steady hands and a face that betrays nothing until the analysis is complete. She cross-references the registry with her own records, mapping the locations of seed vaults and tissue archives across the city.

'Seventeen storage locations,' she says finally. 'Eleven of them are standard corporate vaults — high security, impossible to crack without a team and resources we don't have. Four are satellite offices with moderate security. And two...' She zooms in on the map. 'Two are dead storage. Off-the-books facilities where Meridian stashed assets they've forgotten about. Minimal security. No active monitoring.'

She looks at you with an expression that combines hope and calculation. 'Each of those dead storage sites contains seed samples from the Verdant Reaches. Species we don't have in our collection. If we can access them...'

One of the dead storage sites is in the Scrap Yards. The other is beneath the Transit Hub. Both are reachable through the underground tunnel network. Both are, technically, within your capacity to infiltrate.
'This changes everything,' Maelis says. 'With those samples, we could double our species count. We could rebuild not just a garden, but an ecosystem.'

choices.text: Plan the Scrap Yards raid
Plan the Transit Hub raid

welf_058.title: Private Analysis
text: You take the data back to your room and study it on the clean datapad Maelis gave you. The registry is extensive — Meridian's biological assets span dozens of categories, from crop genetics to pharmaceutical compounds to something labeled 'Heritage Genomics: Non-Human.'

Under Heritage Genomics, you find a sub-project you've never heard of: Project Thornwall. The description is clinical and terrifying: 'Acquisition and analysis of genetic material from Verdant Reaches survivors for application in bioengineered rapid-growth flora. Goal: develop proprietary fast-growth timber strain incorporating elven genetic markers for enhanced photosynthesis and root network communication.'

You read it twice. Three times. The words don't change. Meridian burned your forest, saved the seeds, and is now trying to engineer a new version of your trees using genetic material harvested from your people. A corporate forest. Proprietary. Patented. The trees would grow, but they would belong to Meridian. The Whisper Web would speak, but it would speak in corporate code.
They want to steal everything. Not just the land, not just the trees, but the biology of your species. They want to make a forest that doesn't need elves.

choices.text: Take this to Maelis — this changes everything

welf_059.title: Council of Two
text: Maelis listens to everything — the extraction teams, the list, the Thornwall project — with the focused stillness of a predator assessing a threat. When you finish, she stands and walks to the silverleaf seedling. She touches its leaves, and the bioluminescence in her fingertips flares in response.

'They want to build a forest without us,' she says. 'A corporate ecosystem using our genetics as raw material. The trees would grow, but they would be products. Property. The Whisper Web would be a data network they could monetize.'

She turns to face you. 'This accelerates our timeline. We can't spend fifty years growing in secret if Meridian is actively harvesting our people for genetic material. We need to act faster. We need more allies. And we need to make sure the real forest — our forest, grown from real seeds by real elves singing real songs — gets established before Meridian can patent the fundamental biology of our species.'

The weight of what she's saying settles on you. This isn't just about preservation anymore. This is a race. Maelis's underground garden versus Meridian's corporate bioengineering program. The soul of a forest versus the profit margin of a corporation.
'We need to go public,' she says. 'Not yet. But soon. And when we do, we need to be ready for war.'

choices.text: "Then we prepare for war. What do we need?"
"We need allies. More than just us."

welf_060.title: The Warning
text: You spend the next six hours tracking down every elf on the list. Six of the twelve are in their homes — you knock on doors, show the list, speak the old tongue to prove your identity. Their reactions range from disbelief to terror. One elderly elf, a woman named Vela who was a healer in the Verdant Reaches, simply nods and says, 'I wondered when they'd come for the rest of us.'
Three are at work — you leave coded messages in the old tongue at their residences. Two you can't find; their addresses are wrong or they've moved. The twelfth name is marked 'ACQUIRED.'

You stand in the rain outside the last address and consider what 'ACQUIRED' means. Somewhere in this city, an elf has been taken. For their blood, for their genetics, for the molecular heritage of a forest that burned eighty-seven years ago. And a corporation has filed the acquisition as a line item, the same way they once filed the forest as carbon credits.

The rage is familiar. It's the Thornguard rage, the fire that burned in you when you fought back against the surveyors. But it's older now, colder, more focused. You're not a forty-year-old fighting for a living forest. You're a hundred-and-twenty-seven-year-old fighting for the last fragments of an extinct one.

choices.text: Report to Maelis

welf_061.title: Sera in the Garden
text: Sera follows you through the city's corridors in terrified silence. She doesn't ask questions — the city has taught her that questions are dangerous and answers are expensive. She moves like a city elf, head down, hood up, invisible. It breaks your heart.

When you lead her down the stairs beneath the Jade Lantern and the green light hits her face, she stops. Her eyes go wide. Her lips part. And a sound comes out of her that you haven't heard from a wood elf in decades — a low, unconscious hum on a frequency that matches the growth pattern of living chlorophyll. The nature-sense. She has it. Born in the camps, raised in concrete, and she still carries the biological connection to growing things that is the birthright of every wood elf.

She walks into the basement garden and her bioluminescence activates — faintly, uncertainly, like a light that's been in darkness so long it's forgotten how to be bright. She touches the ferns with trembling fingers. She kneels beside the moss and presses her palm flat against it, and she begins to cry.
'I didn't know,' she whispers. 'I didn't know there was anything left. I thought it was all gone.'
Maelis appears from the far chamber and looks at you. You look at her. Another seed saved. Another fragment of the forest, preserved in the body of a girl who didn't know what she carried.

choices.text: Introduce Sera to Maelis and the plan

welf_062.title: Political Investigation
text: You use your council access to pull every public record related to Project Thornwall. The paper trail is thin — Meridian has classified most of the project details — but you find enough to build a picture. Budget allocations labeled 'Heritage Genomics R&D.' Equipment requisitions for genetic sequencing hardware. Personnel transfers from Meridian's BioDiv department to an unlisted facility.

And buried in an environmental impact report that nobody bothered to redact: a brief mention of 'accelerated reforestation trials using proprietary bio-adapted flora.' They're testing their corporate forest. Somewhere outside the city, Meridian is growing trees engineered from stolen elven genetics.

You take your findings to a journalist named Rik Torres — a human who writes for an independent news feed that covers corporate malfeasance. He's interested but cautious. 'This is a big corporation,' he says. 'I need sources. Documents. Something I can verify independently.'

You can give him the documents. But doing so will draw attention. If Meridian traces the leak, the path leads back to the council, and from there to you, and from you to Maelis and the underground garden. Every move in the political game risks the thing you're trying to protect.

choices.text: Give Torres the documents — the truth must come out
Hold back — protect the garden first

welf_063.title: The Protection Framework
text: You draft a proposal for the council: the Non-Human Biological Autonomy Act. It's a legal framework that would classify the genetic material of non-human species as sovereign property, preventing corporations from harvesting, sequencing, or patenting biological material without explicit consent.
