It's ambitious. It's probably unpassable. But it puts the concept into the political bloodstream.

The council debate lasts three sessions. You speak with the controlled passion of an elf who has learned to translate ancient grief into bureaucratic language. You cite the Verdant Reaches as precedent — a biological heritage destroyed by corporate action, the survivors left without legal protection for even their own DNA.

The proposal is voted down eight to three. But two of the dissenting votes come from representatives who approach you afterward. One is a human rights lawyer named Joseff who lost his family to a corporate medical experiment. The other is an orc community leader named Krath who sees the parallels with the Gene Riots too clearly to ignore.
Three allies. In a city run by corporations, three is a beginning.

choices.text: Build on these new alliances

welf_064.title: The Scrap Yards Raid
text: The Scrap Yards are the city's digestive system — miles of open ground where decommissioned technology, demolished structures, and industrial waste are broken down for recyclable components. The dead storage facility is a bunker buried beneath a mountain of scrapped transport vehicles, accessible only through a maintenance tunnel that hasn't been serviced in decades.

You go at night, when the scavenger gangs that pick through the yards are at their most active. Paradoxically, the chaos of a dozen illegal salvage operations provides perfect cover — the security sensors register so many trespassers that one more elf slipping through the tunnel network is invisible in the noise.

The bunker door yields to Switch's electronic lockpicks. Inside: rows of cryogenic storage units, most dark and dead, their contents degraded beyond recovery. But six units still have power — connected to an independent geothermal tap that has kept them running for decades without human attention.

Inside the six units: seed samples from seventeen Verdant Reaches species not in Maelis's collection. Three of them are species you thought were mythological — plants so rare that even the elves only knew them from the oldest songs. And yet here they are, frozen, patient, waiting. Seeds that have outlived the forest that bore them.

choices.text: Bring the seeds to Maelis

welf_065.title: The Transit Hub Vault
text: The Transit Hub is the city's circulatory system — a massive interchange where maglev trains, transport platforms, and cargo shuttles converge in a controlled chaos of movement and sound. Below the public levels, the infrastructure descends deep into the earth, and somewhere in that mechanical underworld sits a dead storage vault that Meridian has forgotten.

You navigate the sub-levels through maintenance corridors, following Switch's annotated map and your own elven night vision. The deeper you go, the older the infrastructure becomes — from sleek modern alloys to rough concrete to, eventually, natural stone. The vault is carved into bedrock, a pre-city chamber repurposed for storage.

The vault's environmental controls are still active, running on a fusion cell that will outlast the city itself. Inside, you find not just seeds but something that makes your hands shake: a complete soil ecosystem preserved in a sealed terrarium. The label reads 'Verdant Reaches — Sector 7 — Core Sample #1.' This is the actual soil from the forest. Not reconstructed, not synthesized. The real earth, with its real mycorrhizal networks, real bacterial colonies, real worm casings. The bones of your homeland, preserved in glass.
You carry it out like a reliquary. Because that's what it is.

choices.text: Bring the soil to Maelis

welf_066.title: Preparing for What Comes
text: The underground garden grows. With the new seeds, the soil sample, and the intelligence from the registry, Maelis's operation expands from one basement to a network of connected chambers beneath the Market District. The silverleaf seedling is now three feet tall, and its roots have found the remnants of the ancient root network buried deep in the city's foundations.

The Whisper Web stirs. Not alive — not yet — but twitching, like a muscle that's been still for decades and is remembering how to flex. The elves who visit the garden feel it. Sera, with her untrained nature-sense, describes it as 'a humming in the floor.' You, with Sylvara's training, recognize it for what it is: a beginning.

But Meridian is close. Project Thornwall has accelerated — you've heard rumors of corporate agents in the lower districts, asking questions about elven communities, offering cash for blood samples. The extraction teams are still operating, though your warning has driven the marked elves into hiding.
The walls are closing in. The garden needs more time to grow. You need more time to build defenses. But time is the one resource that even an elf can run out of.

choices.text: Focus on protecting the garden
Focus on exposing Meridian
Focus on finding more elves to join the cause

welf_067.title: Building Alliances
text: You cast the net wider. Not just elves — anyone who has reason to fear corporate biological exploitation. The orcs remember the Gene Riots, when Meridian experimented on orc prisoners. The synths know that their code can be rewritten by their manufacturers on a whim. Even some humans — the ones who've seen the cost of corporate immortality technology — recognize the pattern.

You build a coalition quietly, one conversation at a time. A mutual aid network that shares resources, intelligence, and protection. You never mention the underground garden. You never mention the seeds. But you create a shield of community around the Market District that would make it very dangerous for Meridian to operate openly.

Dara becomes your closest ally above ground. She doesn't know everything, but she knows enough to trust you. 'You're building something,' she says one evening, watching you work the room at a community meeting. 'Something bigger than a neighborhood watch. I can't see the shape of it yet, but I can feel it growing.'
'Growing,' you say, and the word tastes like the old tongue. 'Yes. That's exactly what it's doing.'

choices.text: Continue preparations

welf_068.title: The Garden's Shield
text: You implement Maelis's natural countermeasures. The foxfire moss is cultivated along every approach to the garden, its electromagnetic interference creating a dead zone in Meridian's sensor network. You map escape routes through the deep tunnels — paths that lead to chambers so far below the city that no human technology can reach them.

You plant warning systems: fungal networks that respond to unusual vibrations, trained to pulse specific patterns when heavy equipment or multiple footfalls approach. An organic alarm system, living and invisible to electronic detection.

And you recruit. Carefully. Two more elves join the garden — an elder named Orin who was an herbalist in the Verdant Reaches, and a young elf named Taya who has an instinctive talent for the growing songs despite never having learned them formally. With four singers, the growth rate accelerates. The silverleaf puts out new branches. The root network extends.

At night, alone in the garden, you press your hand against the Great Root cutting and you feel the Whisper Web pulse. Faintly. Weakly. But unmistakably. It's coming back. Slowly, impossibly, the forest is remembering how to speak.

choices.text: The crisis arrives — respond

welf_069.title: The Exposure
text: You leak the Project Thornwall documents to Rik Torres through three layers of anonymity. He publishes a series of articles in the independent news feed: 'MERIDIAN HARVESTING ELF DNA FOR PROPRIETARY FOREST.' The story explodes across the lower and middle city networks. For three days, it's all anyone talks about.

Meridian's PR team responds with practiced efficiency: deny, deflect, discredit. They call the story fabricated, accuse Torres of anti-corporate bias, threaten legal action. Their lawyers file injunctions. Their lobbyists pressure the news feed's advertisers. Within a week, the story is buried under a wave of corporate counter-narrative.

But the damage is done. The elf community is galvanized. The phrase 'Thornwall' becomes a rallying cry. Graffiti appears on Meridian subsidiary buildings: 'OUR BLOOD IS NOT YOUR PRODUCT.' Community groups organize protests. For the first time in decades, the wood elf diaspora has a shared cause.

And Meridian is watching. The exposure has made them cautious — the extraction teams pull back, the project goes deeper underground. But it has also made them angry. Corporations have long memories and longer reach, and you've just painted a target on the elf community.

choices.text: The crisis arrives — respond

welf_070.title: The Scattered Seeds
text: You search the elf community with renewed purpose, looking for those who still carry the old skills — the nature-songs, the Remembering, the ability to interface with the Whisper Web. Most of the elders who knew these things are dead or Hollowed. But you find fragments. Pieces.

A woman in Sector 9 who weaves living fiber from synthetic materials, unconsciously applying the old growth-songs to industrial polymers. A teenager in the Neon Strip who can make flowers bloom from cracks in the concrete, though he thinks it's a genetic quirk rather than an ancient elven gift. An old man in the Scrap Yards who hasn't spoken in years but hums the melodies of the deep-forest Remembering whenever he sleeps.

Each of them carries a piece of the puzzle. None of them have the complete picture. The traditions were transmitted through centuries of apprenticeship in a living forest, and without the forest, the transmission broke. The skills fragmented, scattered across a diaspora, held by individuals who don't know what they carry.

You begin, quietly, to teach. To reconnect. To weave the fragments back together. It will take years. You have years. You are the patient species, and patience is the last weapon the city hasn't taken from you.

choices.text: The crisis arrives — respond

welf_071.title: The Raid
text: It happens on a Tuesday. No warning. No preamble. Just the sudden shriek of the fungal alarm network pulsing danger patterns through the garden's root system, and Maelis's voice on the comm: 'They're coming. Three teams. Northeast tunnel.'

Meridian corporate security. Someone talked, or someone was followed, or their sensor technology finally cracked the foxfire interference. It doesn't matter how. What matters is that twelve armed operatives are moving through the tunnels toward the garden, and everything you've built — the seeds, the saplings, the silverleaf, the Great Root cutting — is in their path.
You have minutes. Not enough time to evacuate everything, but enough time to make a choice.

choices.text: Fight — buy time for Maelis to evacuate the seeds
Evacuate — save what you can and flee to the deep tunnels
Activate the foxfire countermeasures — blind them with EMP
Collapse the tunnel — bury the entrance

welf_072.title: The Last Thornguard
text: You told Sylvara, once, that you would never fight again. That the burning had taught you that violence was futile against an enemy with unlimited resources. She looked at you with her ancient eyes and said, 'Violence is always futile in the long term. But in the short term, sometimes it's the only thing standing between a seed and a boot.'

You take up position in the northeast tunnel, alone. The corridor is narrow — only room for two abreast — which negates their numerical advantage. You have darkness, elven night vision, a monofilament blade, and the cold fury of someone defending the last forest on earth.

The first two go down before they know you're there. The monofilament cuts through their armor like it's cloth. The third fires wildly in the dark, his muzzle flash lighting you up for a fraction of a second — long enough for the fourth to get a shot off. Pain blooms in your left shoulder, hot and sharp. You don't stop. You can't stop.

Behind you, Maelis works frantically, loading the seed vault into portable containers. Sera and Orin carry the cryogenic units. Taya sings a desperate growing-song to the silverleaf, coaxing it to release its roots from the soil so it can be transported.

You hold the tunnel for seven minutes. Seven minutes of blood and shadow and the old War Songs that haven't been sung in eighty-seven years. Seven minutes that cost you a shoulder wound, two cracked ribs, and a portion of your soul that you'll never get back.

choices.text: Retreat to the deep tunnels with the others

welf_073.title: Tactical Retreat
text: No heroics. No last stands. You've watched enough elves die for pride. 'Evacuate,' you tell Maelis. 'Deep tunnel route. Now.'

The protocol activates like a drill you've rehearsed — because you have rehearsed it, every week, for exactly this scenario. Maelis grabs the seed vault. You take the silverleaf seedling, wrapping its pot in insulating fabric. Sera loads the soil samples. Orin and Taya handle the Great Root cutting with the reverence it deserves.

You move through the escape tunnel at speed, using the fungal network to track the raiders' progress behind you. They reach the garden three minutes after you leave. You hear the crash of equipment being overturned, the shatter of grow lights, the sharp reports of weapons fire into empty rooms.

They find nothing but ferns and moss. The seeds are with you. The silverleaf is with you. The Great Root is with you. The garden is lost, but the garden was always just a place. The forest is in the seeds, and the seeds are in your hands.
You descend into the deep tunnels, where the stone is old and the silence is absolute, and you begin again.

choices.text: Establish the new garden in the deep tunnels

welf_074.title: Foxfire Storm
text: The foxfire moss was designed for exactly this purpose. You trigger the activation sequence — a specific frequency hummed in the old tongue — and the moss responds. Electromagnetic interference erupts through the tunnel network like a biological EMP, frying every electronic device within a hundred yards. The raiders' comm units die. Their tactical displays go dark. Their weapon-mounted targeting systems fail. In the absolute blackness of the underground, they are blind.
You are not. Elven night vision is biological, not electronic. In the dark, you are the apex predator of these tunnels, and the raiders are stumbling prey.

You don't kill them. You don't need to. In the foxfire darkness, you and Maelis move the critical assets — seeds, silverleaf, Great Root cutting — through side tunnels the raiders can't navigate. Sera guides the fox-spirits through narrow passages the humans can't fit through. By the time the raiders' backup equipment comes online, you're in the deep tunnels, beyond the range of their sensors.
The foxfire burns itself out in thirty minutes. But thirty minutes is an eternity when you're an elf in the dark with a head start and a network of tunnels that humans have never mapped.

choices.text: Regroup in the deep tunnels

welf_075.title: Burying the Way
text: The tunnel supports are old, weakened by decades of neglect. You identified the stress points months ago, during one of your security assessments. Three key supports, and the entire northeast corridor collapses.

You sing the old breaking-song — the inverse of the growth-songs, a melody that convinces material to let go of its structure, to surrender its cohesion. The elders used it to clear deadfall in the forest, persuading fallen trees to decompose faster so new growth could take root. Applied to corroded metal supports in a pre-city tunnel, the effect is dramatic.

The supports groan, twist, and fail. Tons of concrete and stone cascade into the corridor with a roar that shakes the garden. Dust billows. The raiders on the other side are sealed off, buried behind a wall of rubble that would take heavy machinery weeks to clear.

But the collapse also seals the garden's main entrance. You're trapped on the inside, with the seeds and the silverleaf and the team, and the only way out is down — into the deep tunnels that Maelis has mapped but never fully explored.
'Forward, then,' Maelis says, hoisting the seed vault. 'We always knew this place was temporary. The forest moves on.'

choices.text: Descend into the deep tunnels

welf_076.title: The Deep
text: The deep tunnels are older than the city. Older than human civilization. Natural cave systems carved by water over geological time, lined with mineral deposits that glitter in the light of your bioluminescence. The air is clean — filtered through miles of limestone, it's the purest atmosphere you've breathed since the forest. And it's warm, heated by geothermal activity that turns the deepest chambers into something almost tropical.

You find a cavern large enough to house the new garden. The ceiling is high enough for trees to grow. A natural water source trickles from a fissure in the rock, and the mineral content of the soil is — you can feel it through your bare feet — rich with the nutrients that forest ecology needs.

Maelis plants the silverleaf seedling in the new soil, and it takes root with a speed that makes everyone gasp. The old nature-songs echo off the cavern walls in harmonics that the stone seems to amplify, and the seedling responds. It grows a full inch in an hour. In the forest, that would be normal. In a cave beneath a concrete city, it's a miracle.

The Great Root cutting goes into the deepest soil. And this time, when you press your hand to the earth above it, the Whisper Web doesn't just pulse. It speaks. A single word, in a frequency below hearing, transmitted through stone and soil and root: Grow.

choices.text: This is the place. This is where the forest returns.

welf_077.title: The New Root
text: Days pass. The garden takes shape in the deep cavern with a speed that borders on the supernatural. The silverleaf grows. Other seedlings sprout from the stolen seeds. The soil ecosystem, transplanted from the core sample, spreads through the cavern floor like a living carpet. Fox-spirits explore the cave system's side passages and begin denning in the warm hollows near the geothermal vents.

Maelis works with a focused intensity you recognize from the old days — the same look she wore when she wove vine crowns in the canopy, absolute concentration channeled through an unshakeable belief that growing things is the only answer that matters.

You divide your time between the garden and the surface. Above, the aftermath of the raid plays out: Meridian security scours the tunnel network but finds only the abandoned basement, stripped of evidence. Hana's tea house is questioned and released — her cover holds. The community network you built rallies, providing shelter and resources for the displaced elves.
Below, the forest grows. And each time you descend from the concrete world to the green one, the transition feels less like entering a cave and more like coming home.
But you know this peace is temporary. Meridian will not stop. Project Thornwall will not stop. The world above grinds on, and you are needed in it.

choices.text: Prepare to return to the surface

welf_078.title: Maelis's Farewell
text: The night before you return to the surface, Maelis finds you sitting beside the silverleaf, your hand resting on its trunk. The tree is six feet tall now and growing. Its silver-edged leaves catch your bioluminescence and multiply it, filling the cavern with a soft, living light.
'I'm staying below,' Maelis says. 'The garden needs a keeper. But you — you were always meant for the upper world. You have the ability to move between both. To be the bridge.'

She sits beside you. For a moment, you're both forty again, sitting in the canopy of the Verdant Reaches, the Whisper Web humming beneath your feet, the future an infinite green landscape stretching beyond imagination. Then the moment passes, and you're in a cave, and the forest is a handful of saplings, and the future is uncertain and dangerous and absolutely worth fighting for.
'When you go up,' Maelis says, 'remember what Sylvara taught us. We are the seeds. The concrete cannot last forever. And when it crumbles, we will be ready.'

She presses something into your hand. A second silverleaf seed, glowing faintly. 'In case everything goes wrong. In case they find us again. In case the worst happens. Carry this, and the forest can never truly die.'

choices.text: Embrace her and prepare for the surface

welf_079.title: The Surface
text: You emerge into the Lower District at dawn. The acid rain has stopped, and through a gap in the cloud cover, a thin blade of actual sunlight cuts between the megastructures and touches the wet concrete of Sector 7. You stand in it, eyes closed, and for a second the warmth on your face is indistinguishable from sunlight filtered through a forest canopy.
The city is the same. The concrete, the neon, the drone patrols, the press of too many bodies in too little space. Nothing has changed in the world above. But everything has changed in you.

You carry two silverleaf seeds. You carry the knowledge of a garden growing in the deep places. You carry the memory of ten thousand years of forest and the hope of ten thousand more. You carry Sylvara's teaching and Maelis's plan and the sound of the Whisper Web speaking for the first time in eighty-seven years.

The city doesn't know it yet, but a forest is growing beneath its foundations. And you are the one who walks above, the bridge between concrete and canopy, the last Thornguard of a new Verdant Reaches that exists — for now — in darkness and in hope.
Your datapad buzzes. A message from a number you don't recognize. 'Meet at Mako's Repair Shop. Something you need to see.' Time to begin.

choices.text: Face the future as a Guardian — strength and duty above all
Face the future as a Diplomat — build bridges, not walls
Face the future as a Shadow — protect the garden from the dark
Face the future as a Keeper — carry the old ways into the new world

welf_080.title: The Elder's Song
text: In the quiet hours of the deep garden, you practice Sylvara's songs. The melodies were designed for a forest — for open air and living wood and the vast amplifying chamber of a canopy that stretched for miles. In the cave, they bounce off stone and come back changed, acquiring harmonics that Sylvara never intended but that seem, somehow, right.

The cave adds its own voice. The drip of water becomes percussion. The hum of the geothermal vents becomes a bass note. The stone resonates with frequencies that make the growing things respond, and you realize something that shifts your understanding of everything: the forest doesn't need a specific place. The forest is a set of relationships — between root and soil, between water and stone, between song and growth. The place can change. The relationships endure.

This is what Sylvara was trying to tell you. Not 'we are seeds' as metaphor, but as fact. The forest is not a location. The forest is a pattern. And patterns can be replicated anywhere, as long as someone remembers the shape.
You remember the shape. You will always remember the shape.

choices.text: Continue preparing for the surface

welf_081.title: Fox-Spirit Dawn
text: You wake to a warm weight on your chest. A fox-spirit kit — the youngest of the litter, barely bigger than your hand — has curled up on you during the night. Its fur shifts colors in the bioluminescent light, rippling through shades of amber and copper and the deep red of autumn leaves. In the forest, fox-spirits were symbiotic companions to the elves. They carried messages through the Whisper Web, tended the mushroom gardens, and sang harmonies with the growth-songs that no other species could match.

The kit opens one eye, regards you with ancient intelligence, and chirps a three-note melody that you recognize. It's the dawn greeting — the song fox-spirits sang every morning in the Verdant Reaches, a confirmation that the forest had survived another night.

The forest. This cave, these saplings, these handful of survivors — this is the forest now. And the fox-spirits know it. They've adapted, the way all living things adapt, finding the pattern of home in a new shape.
You scratch behind the kit's ears and it purrs like a small motor. For the first time in eighty-seven years, waking up doesn't hurt.

choices.text: Carry this warmth into the day's work

welf_082.title: Thandel's Visit
text: You bring Thandel to the deep garden on a night when the geothermal vents are warm and the bioluminescence is at its brightest. He comes silently, led by your hand through the tunnels, his empty eyes seeing nothing. He hasn't spoken in eighty-seven years. The counselors say his mind is trapped in the dead Whisper Web, endlessly searching for a signal that no longer exists.

You bring him to the Great Root cutting and place his hand on the bark. For ten seconds, nothing happens. Then his fingers tighten. His pupils dilate. His lips part, and a sound comes from his throat — a sound he hasn't made since the burning. A hum. The frequency of the Whisper Web.

The Great Root responds. A pulse of warmth spreads from the cutting through the soil, and every plant in the cavern shivers. The silverleaf's leaves rustle without wind. The moss brightens. And Thandel's eyes — empty for decades — focus.
He looks at you. He sees you. And in a voice cracked by eighty-seven years of disuse, he says one word: 'Home.'
You hold him while he weeps. The Whisper Web hums around you both, thin and fragile and alive.

choices.text: Welcome Thandel back to the world

welf_083.title: The Naming Ceremony
text: Maelis insists on a naming ceremony for the new garden. In the Verdant Reaches, every grove had a name sung into the Whisper Web, a vibrational signature that identified it as uniquely as a fingerprint. Without a name, the garden is just a collection of plants in a cave. With one, it becomes a place. A home. A node in a network that extends backward through ten thousand years of history.

The four of you — you, Maelis, Sera, Orin — stand in a circle around the silverleaf and sing the Naming Song in four-part harmony. The old tongue resonates in the stone cavern, acquiring overtones and undertones that wrap around the central melody like vines around a trunk.

Maelis chose the name: Thar'vael. In the old tongue, it means 'Root-Heart.' The place where the new network begins. The first beat of a pulse that will, if everything goes right, eventually spread through the deep places of the earth like a second circulatory system for the planet.
When the song ends, the Whisper Web carries the name into the soil, and the silverleaf's leaves glow with a light you haven't seen since the Verdant Reaches. The forest acknowledges its newest grove.
Thar'vael. The Root-Heart. It exists. It is real. It is growing.

choices.text: Carry the name with you to the surface

welf_084.title: Night Watch
text: You take the night watch in the deep tunnels, sitting at the junction where the main passage meets the approach to Thar'vael. The stone is warm beneath you. The distant hum of the geothermal vents is like breathing. In the darkness beyond your bioluminescent glow, the tunnel stretches into the unknown.

You think about the math. Maelis's plan requires centuries. The silverleaf needs fifty years to reach maturity. The root network needs a century to establish itself. A full underground ecosystem — self-sustaining, capable of producing clean air and water — is a three-hundred-year project at minimum.

You'll live to see it. That's the terrible gift of elven longevity. You'll live to see it, or you'll live to see it fail. Either way, you'll be there, counting the years in growth rings and seasonal cycles that only elves can perceive in the underground dark.

A fox-spirit trots past your position, pauses to touch its nose to your knee, and continues on its patrol. It knows the tunnels better than you do. In the dark, in the deep, the old partnerships still hold. The forest remembers how to watch over its own.

choices.text: Dawn approaches — prepare for the surface

welf_085.title: The Old Knife
text: Before you leave for the surface, you sharpen your knife on a whetstone Orin produced from his pack. The blade is still good steel despite the rotting handle. You've carried it for decades — it was the first tool you acquired in the city, traded for a day's labor at a recycling plant. The tape around the handle is grimy and fraying.

Sera watches you work. 'In the old stories,' she says, 'the Thornguard carried blades grown from living wood. Ironwood, hard as metal but alive. The trees gave their branches for the defense of the forest.'
'Those stories are true,' you say. 'I carried one. It burned with the rest.'

Sera is quiet for a moment. Then she reaches into the soil bed beside the silverleaf and, with an instinct that owes nothing to training and everything to genetic memory, sings a three-note phrase in the old tongue. A tendril of ironwood — one of the seedlings from the stolen collection — extends a branch toward your knife. The branch wraps around the taped handle, bonding with the metal, growing into the gaps and seams until the knife has a handle of living wood.
You stare at it. Sera stares at it. She didn't know she could do that. But the forest did.

choices.text: Thank Sera and prepare to leave

welf_086.title: Orin's Medicine
text: Orin, the old herbalist, has been experimenting with the Verdant Reaches plants in the deep garden. His workspace is a natural alcove in the cavern wall, lined with drying racks and distillation equipment he built from scavenged parts. The air around his station smells of green and bitter things — the concentrated essence of plants that haven't existed in the wild for nearly a century.

'The moonvine produces a compound,' he tells you, crushing leaves between his fingers with the practiced ease of seven hundred years of herbalism. 'In the forest, we used it for healing. Minor wounds, infections, the kind of everyday injuries that city medicine handles with synthetics. But down here, with the geothermal soil and the Whisper Web pulse, the moonvine is producing a variant I've never seen. The healing compound is ten times more concentrated.'

He hands you a small vial of pale blue liquid. 'Take this to the surface. Use it sparingly — a single drop can close a wound in minutes. And if you find yourself in the kind of trouble that needs more than a drop, come back to me.'
Elven medicine, grown in the deep garden, powered by the pulse of a reborn Whisper Web. The old ways adapting to the new world. Sylvara would have wept.

choices.text: Take the medicine and prepare for the surface

welf_087.title: The Map of Roots
text: Maelis has been mapping the deep tunnel network for years, but the Great Root cutting reveals something her maps couldn't: an ancient root system, petrified and dormant, that extends for miles beneath the city. The original Verdant Reaches didn't end at the forest's edge. Its roots went deep — deeper than anyone knew — and the petrified remnants form a vast underground network that the city was built on top of without ever knowing.

If the Great Root cutting can connect to these ancient channels, the new Whisper Web won't just grow in Thar'vael. It will spread through the entire sub-foundation of the city, following the ghost-roads of roots that last carried water ten thousand years ago.

The implications are staggering. A living root network beneath a megacity. Trees that could grow in any basement, any tunnel, any crack deep enough to reach the old channels. A forest that doesn't replace the city but grows through it, under it, around it, until the concrete and the canopy are inseparable.
'This is bigger than either of us imagined,' Maelis whispers, staring at the scan data. 'This is bigger than the Verdant Reaches ever was.'

choices.text: Carry this knowledge to the surface

welf_088.title: Sera's Song
text: Sera has been learning the old tongue from you and Orin, absorbing it with the hungry speed of a young elf who has found something she didn't know she was missing. Born in the camps, raised on synthetic food and recycled air, she had no context for the language of her ancestors. Now, in the deep garden, surrounded by living things for the first time in her life, the old tongue flows from her like water finding its level.

She sings a growing-song to the ferns one morning, and the melody she chooses is one you've never heard before. It's not from Sylvara's repertoire, not from the traditional canon. It's new. Sera has composed a growing-song from scratch, in the old tongue, using melodic structures that the language suggested to her through its own internal logic.

The ferns respond. They respond enthusiastically, unfurling new fronds in minutes, reaching for Sera's voice like sunflowers tracking the sun. The growth rate is faster than anything in the traditional songs.

You watch with an emotion you can't name. The old ways aren't just being preserved. They're evolving. The next generation isn't copying the past — they're building on it. The forest isn't being restored. It's being reinvented.
Sylvara would have loved her.

choices.text: Prepare for the surface with renewed hope

welf_089.title: The Weight of Centuries
text: Alone, in a side chamber of Thar'vael, you sit and practice the Remembering. Not the joyful Remembering of the forest, but the full accounting — the complete record of your hundred and twenty-seven years, cataloged and preserved in the elven tradition.

You remember the canopy. The Breathing World. Sylvara's hands on yours, teaching you to feel the Whisper Web. You remember the surveyors. The council. The Thornguard. The burning — nine days of fire and the silence that followed. You remember the camps, the concrete, the eighty-seven years of Hollowing. And you remember this: the silverleaf seedling. Maelis's face. Sera's song. The Whisper Web speaking its first word in nearly a century.

The full weight of it settles on you, and you let it. You don't flinch from it. An elf who cannot carry the weight of their years is an elf who has not lived fully. You carry it all — the grief and the hope, the loss and the recovery, the fire and the growth — and you stand.
One hundred and twenty-seven years. In the forest, barely an adult. In the city, ancient beyond measure. In the deep garden, exactly old enough to know what matters.

choices.text: Rise and go to the surface

welf_090.title: The Commission's Ghost
text: On one of your surface trips, you discover something in the public archives that changes your understanding of the burning. The United Species Commission — the body that was supposed to protect non-human habitats — received a formal appeal from the Verdant Reaches Elder Council ninety-one days before the fire. The appeal invoked the Accord of Living Spaces. It was legally valid. It should have triggered an immediate injunction against Meridian's operations.

The appeal was marked 'RECEIVED.' It was never marked 'PROCESSED.' It sat in a queue for ninety-one days while the bulldozers and the incendiary teams did their work. On the ninety-second day — one day after the fire — it was marked 'MOOT: TERRITORY NO LONGER EXTANT.'

You stare at the record on your datapad screen. Ninety-one days. The council was right. The law was on your side. And someone — a bureaucrat, a functionary, a person whose name is lost in the system's machinery — let the appeal gather dust while a forest burned.

The rage is clean and cold and ancient. Not at Meridian — you've always known what corporations are. But at the system that promised protection and delivered paperwork. At the law that existed and wasn't enforced. At the gap between what civilization promises and what it delivers.

choices.text: Add this to the case against Meridian

welf_091.title: The Market Stall
text: To maintain your surface cover, you return to an old routine: selling small demonstrations of the nature-songs at a market stall. But this time, the demonstrations are different. The plants you work with are Thar'vael-grown — stronger, more responsive, infused with the energy of the reborn Whisper Web. When you coax a flower to bloom from a crack in a customer's concrete wall, it doesn't wilt in hours like it used to. It persists. It grows. It sends tiny roots into the concrete and holds on.

People notice. Word spreads. The elf at the market stall who can make real flowers grow in dead concrete. Customers come from other sectors. An elf from a different diaspora — the Ashwood clan, displaced from a forest two hundred miles north — finds your stall and stares at the growing flowers with tears in her eyes.
'How?' she asks. Just the one word.

You can't tell her everything. Not yet. But you give her a seedling — a common fern, nothing conspicuous — and you teach her the first three notes of the simplest growing-song. It's not much. A fragment of a fragment. But the look on her face when the fern responds to her voice is worth more than anything the market stall has ever earned.

choices.text: Continue building your surface life

welf_092.title: Hana's Confession
text: On a quiet evening in the Jade Lantern — the tea house survived the raid, its cover still intact — Hana pours you a cup of real tea and sits down across from you. Her face carries the weight of something unsaid.

'I need to tell you why I really left Meridian,' she says. 'It wasn't just the seed vault. It wasn't just moral objection. I was the lead researcher on the Verdant Reaches deforestation assessment. I'm the one who filed the viability report. I wrote the words: Recommend full clear and grade.'
The tea cup in your hand doesn't crack. Your hand doesn't shake. But something inside you goes very still.

'I didn't know,' she says, and her voice breaks. 'I didn't understand what I was authorizing. I was twenty-six and I thought it was just trees. I didn't know about the Whisper Web, the symbiosis, the ten thousand years of living culture. I didn't know that clear and grade meant the end of a civilization. By the time I understood, it was too late.'

Silence. The tea steams between you. 'I can't undo it,' she says. 'I can never undo it. But I can spend the rest of my life trying to grow back what I helped destroy. That's why I'm here. That's why I'll always be here.'

choices.text: Forgive her — the forest needs gardeners, not grudges
Say nothing — some truths take time to process

welf_093.title: The Dream of Canopy
text: You dream of the forest. Not the memory — the Remembering produces waking visions, precise and controlled. This is a true dream, unstructured and wild, the kind of dream that the Whisper Web used to send to sleeping elves.

In the dream, you stand in a forest that doesn't exist yet. The trees are massive — silverleaf elms and ironwood oaks stretching hundreds of feet into a sky you can't see through the canopy. But the ground beneath the trees is concrete. The trunks grow from cracks in city streets. The roots have broken through foundations, split retaining walls, crumbled the infrastructure of a dead megacity. Everywhere, green pushes through gray.

Fox-spirits dart through the undergrowth. Elves move through the canopy, singing the old songs in voices that carry harmonics from stone and metal as well as wood. And the Whisper Web — vast, ancient, new — hums beneath everything, connecting it all.

You wake with tears on your face and the heartwood token warm against your chest. A dream. Maybe a prophecy. Maybe the Whisper Web in Thar'vael, reaching up through miles of stone to show you what could be, if the seeds survive. If the songs continue. If the patience holds.

choices.text: Carry the dream into the waking world

welf_094.title: The Burned District
text: On a day when the grief is heavier than usual, you take the transit to the edge of the city and look east, toward where the Verdant Reaches used to be. There's nothing there now but industrial development — processing plants, chemical refineries, and the massive Meridian BioDiv complex where Project Thornwall presumably operates behind locked doors.

But at the very edge, where the last processing plant meets the open ground, you see something that stops your heart. A weed. A single green shoot, pushing up through the toxic soil of the burn zone, defying every environmental analysis that says nothing can grow here for another hundred years.

You kneel beside it. It's not a Verdant Reaches species — just a common ragweed, tough and stubborn and completely ordinary. But it's growing. In the ashes of your homeland, in the poisoned soil of a corporate crime scene, life is finding a way back.
You don't pick it. You don't touch it. You just kneel in the ash and you watch it grow, and you think about patience, and seeds, and the fundamental stubbornness of living things.

choices.text: Return to the city with a quiet heart

welf_095.title: Kael's Lesson
text: On your last training session before committing fully to the surface work, Kael sits you down after the sparring and says something unexpected. 'You fight like someone who's protecting something important. Most people I train fight for money, for reputation, for survival. You fight like a mother over a crib. What are you protecting?'
You consider lying. You consider deflecting. Instead, you say: 'The future.'

He nods slowly. 'Then learn this: the best way to protect something is to make sure nobody knows it exists. The second best way is to make the cost of touching it so high that nobody tries. You're an elf — you can do the first better than any human. But if the first fails, you need to be ready for the second.'

He hands you a card. An address in the Lower District. 'There's a man there who teaches the kind of fighting they don't put in manuals. The kind where you walk in alone and walk out alone and nobody remembers you were there. Tell him I sent you. And elf? Don't die. I'm starting to like you.'

choices.text: Take Kael's card and prepare for the surface

welf_096.title: The Last Night Below
text: Your last night in Thar'vael before the long surface assignment. The garden is beautiful in the bioluminescent dark — a cathedral of green in a world of stone, improbable and defiant and alive. You walk the paths between the saplings, touching each trunk, feeling the pulse of the Whisper Web growing stronger with each passing day.

Maelis finds you at the silverleaf. The tree is taller than you now, and its silver-edged leaves have begun producing the phosphorescent spores that, in the old forest, would drift through the canopy like living stars. In the cave, they float upward and catch in the mineral formations of the ceiling, creating a constellation of soft green light.

You stand together under the artificial stars of a reborn forest and neither of you speaks. There is nothing to say that the trees aren't already saying, in the slow language of growth and root and the patient accumulation of rings.
Tomorrow, the surface. Tomorrow, the concrete and the neon and the long game. But tonight, for one night, you are home.

choices.text: Sleep one last night in the garden

welf_097.title: The Guardian's Beginning
text: You choose the way of the Thornguard. The old oath, adapted for a new world: protect the growing things. Protect the seeds, the saplings, the people who tend them. Stand between the garden and the fire, and do not move.

The city is your forest now. The concrete corridors are the game trails. The hab-block shadows are the canopy shade. The people — all of them, elf and human and orc and synth — are the ecosystem. And you are the guardian, the thorn in the city's side, the silent watcher who ensures that nothing threatens the roots below.

You strap the living-wood knife to your belt. You pocket the silverleaf seed. You press the heartwood token to your lips — Sylvara's last gift, still warm after all these years — and you step into the rain.

Somewhere beneath your feet, a root grows one millimeter deeper into the earth. Somewhere in a cave, a fox-spirit sings the dawn greeting. Somewhere in the Market District, Hana brews real tea for the next elf who walks through her door.
The forest is coming back. And you will fight for it with every breath of every century you have left.
Your story continues in the city above. The message from Mako's awaits.

choices.text: Begin the prologue

welf_098.title: The Diplomat's Beginning
text: You choose the way of the Voice. The old council tradition, adapted for a world that burned the council and the tree they sat under: speak for those who cannot speak for themselves. Build bridges. Build coalitions. Build a wall of law and community so strong that no corporation can burn through it.

The city is your audience now. The political meetings are your council circles. The community networks are your Whisper Web. Every conversation, every alliance, every hard-won legal protection is a root that strengthens the foundation beneath Thar'vael.

You straighten your jacket, pocket the silverleaf seed, and check your reflection in the cracked mirror. The elf who looks back at you is tired, scarred, a hundred and twenty-seven years old and carrying the weight of a dead world. But her eyes are clear. And when she speaks, people listen. They listen because she has lived longer than their institutions, and she brings a patience that outlasts their cycles of outrage and apathy.

Dara, Joseff, Krath — your allies are waiting. The council meets tomorrow. There are proposals to draft, connections to build, a city to slowly, patiently persuade that forests matter even when — especially when — they exist only in the dark places beneath the foundations.
The forest is coming back. And you will speak for it until the city listens.
Your story continues in the city above. The message from Mako's awaits.

choices.text: Begin the prologue

welf_099.title: The Shadow's Beginning
text: You choose the way of the Shadow. The unseen path, the quiet knife, the operative who moves through the city's dark places and ensures that the garden's enemies never see the blow coming. It is not the honorable path. The old Thornguard would not recognize it. But honor is a luxury of people who still have something left to lose, and you have already lost everything except the seeds.

The city is your hunting ground now. The corporate networks are your intelligence sources. The underground economy is your supply chain. You will steal what the garden needs, sabotage what threatens it, and eliminate — with precision and without pleasure — anyone who comes too close to Thar'vael.
Switch's tools sit comfortably in your pockets: the signal scrambler, the lockpicks, the monofilament blade. You add the silverleaf seed to your collection of concealed assets and step into the rain.

In the old forest, there were predators. Silent creatures that moved through the canopy at night, keeping the ecosystem in balance by culling what needed culling. The elves respected them. They didn't love them, but they understood: every garden needs a gardener, and every gardener needs a guard who works in the dark.
The forest is coming back. And you will guard it from the shadows, unseen and unremembered, for as long as shadows exist.
Your story continues in the city above. The message from Mako's awaits.

choices.text: Begin the prologue

welf_100.title: The Keeper's Beginning
text: You choose the way of the Keeper. Sylvara's path. The tradition holder, the memory bearer, the one who ensures that the old ways survive not as artifacts but as living practice. Every song, every ritual, every fragment of the Whisper Web's language — you will carry it, teach it, and pass it on to elves like Sera who never knew the forest but carry it in their blood.

The city is your classroom now. The scattered elves are your students. The dying traditions are your curriculum. You will find every elf who carries a fragment of the old knowledge and weave those fragments back together, rebuilding the cultural infrastructure that the burning destroyed.

You touch the heartwood token — warm, always warm, breathing with a life that defies the concrete that surrounds it — and you speak the old words. The words of the Naming. The words of the Growing. The words that Sylvara taught you and her teacher taught her and her teacher's teacher taught them, back through the generations to the first elf who pressed their hand against the first tree and felt the Whisper Web for the first time.

Ten thousand years of accumulated wisdom, carried in the memory of a single elf in a concrete room. It should be terrifying. It is terrifying. But it is also a purpose, and purpose is the one thing the city cannot take from you.
The forest is coming back. And you will teach it how to sing.
Your story continues in the city above. The message from Mako's awaits.

choices.text: Begin the prologue
